experiments.title: How adding check boxes to a statement of belief impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a checkbox to the acquisition form increase name conversion on the pledge?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/13/2019 - 03/15/2020
Billy Graham Evangelistic Association was running an acquisition offer pledge. People would add their name to this pledge if they aligned with it to show support for the cause. In an effort to increase name conversion on the page, we hypothesized adding a checkbox to the form where people would have to mark if they did, in fact, believe in what they were signing. We split this traffic 50/50 with the control.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By adding the single checkbox to the pledge, name conversion on the page increased by 10.6%!
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Desktop visitors had a 26.5% increase with a 100% level of confidence.Mobile visitors had a 9.1% increase with a 100% level of confidence.
The checkbox increased the motivation of the user. It brought confirmation to why they were on the page and to their beliefs. This simple tactic can have a significant impact not only on name conversion as we saw with this experiment, but on donor conversion as well since more people will be getting to the instant donation page presented immediately after a person signs the pledge.

experiments.title: How bringing clarity to the donation form impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we bring clarity to the donation process by adding steps to the donation form and communicating with people what they need to do?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/22/2020 - 08/13/2020
Billy Graham Evangelistic Association offers a free evangelism guide called Simply Sharing Jesus. After a person signs up for this offer, the confirmation page is a donation page where people can also become a donor, instantly. The instant donor conversion on this page was healthy, but we hypothesized that the donation form and process may be causing friction to the conversion process. We hypothesized that if we clearly communicated to people exactly what they needed to do by making steps within the form, that we might be able to increase donor conversion on the page. We split the traffic 50/50 to test this.
experiments.keyLearnings: The stepped donation process increased donor conversion by 31%! This significant increase in donor conversion reinforces the hypothesis that there WAS friction on the donation form. When people can see the steps they need to take to make a gift, and are told how to do it, we can increase donor conversion on the page. The increase in donations was so significant that it resulted in a 19% increase in overall revenue for the organization from this page.

experiments.title: How increasing perceived security on a primary donation page for mobile devices can impact donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding visual elements increase the perception of form security increase donor conversion on mobile devices?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/26/2019 - 03/26/2019
One of the most common reasons web visitors don’t complete transactions online is a perceived lack of security. Knowing this, Billy Graham Evangelical Association wondered if their primary donation pathway was losing potential donors because it didn’t visually represent how secure it was. They created a special security box around the credit card section of form and added a padlock into the shaded box to represent that the form was secure. This didn’t make the form more secure—it was already PCI compliant and had the highest levels of security. All these design tweaks did was increase perception of security with the end user. They launched an A/B test on all device types (desktop/mobile/tablet) to see if it increased donor conversion as they hypothesized.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Adding visual elements to increase the perception of security on the donation form decreased donor conversion by 16% on mobile devices. We hypothesize that this decrease could be in the way that the visual elements look on mobile. Instead of reducing friction for the donor pathway on mobile devices, the visual security elements may have actually increased this by making the credit card section stand out from the rest of the elements on the form, drawing attention to it being called out.
Even though we saw a 16% decrease in donor conversion on mobile devices, we saw a 20% increase in donor conversion on desktop devices. While it is important to look at the impact on all devices, it is important to know that the majority of donations and revenue (67%) on the primary donation page comes from desktop devices. Because the revenue impact is so much greater on desktop than on other devices, we recommend rolling out this tactic on desktop to continue to increase donations and revenue, while no longer featuring the tactic on mobile to eliminate the negative impact.

experiments.title: How removing address fields on an acquisition page impacts name and donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would removing address fields from an acquisition form improve conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/16/2019 - 09/20/2019
Billy Graham Evangelical Association offered people a free eBook called Simply Sharing Jesus. The eBook acquisition form asked people to provide their name, email, and full address in order to get the eBook. Since this is strictly a digital offer, we hypothesized that asking for less information from people up front might improve name acquisition from the form. We developed a treatment that removed the address field from the form and only required a person’s name and email address. We used paid Facebook ads to push prospective names to the acquisition page and split the traffic 50/50 through Google Optimize.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We increased name acquisition by 9%! By removing these fields and only asking for the most pertinent information required to get the offer, we decreased friction on the page to get the offer. In addition to this, this experiment also had an even greater impact on donor conversion. After a person signed up for the eBook, they were then presented with an instant donation ask. We found that by removing the address field from the initial step in the acquisition funnel we were able to increase donor conversion by 36%! The validity of those results was 86% , but the lift is directional and can tell us a lot:
Asking for the right amount and kind of information at the appropriate step in an acquisition funnel can have a significant impact on name AND donor conversion.By delaying to ask for a person's address will move more people through the initial funnel, increasing the number of people available to make a donation, and increasing the motivation of these people overall so that at the time of an ask, they were more likely to give.

rows.name: From the Desk of Franklin Graham Control
From the Desk of Franklin Graham Treatment

experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase donor conversion with a radical, simplified version of the email?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/29/2019
Billy Graham Evangelical Association send out a monthly email called, “From the Desk of Franklin Graham”. This email serves two purposes – one, to ask people to pray, and two, to ask people to make a gift. When we analyzed what was currently being sent out, we hypothesized that a more simplified approach to the email may increase conversion. We hypothesized that making the email look and feel like it was actually from Franklin Graham may increase engagement and ultimately conversion. To do this, we removed the template of the email and made it more plain text, removed the headline, image, CTA’s buttons, and social sharing options. We then moved the premium off up into the main part of the body copy to connect the donation ask to the offer. No kept the same body copy of the email and didn’t add or change any of it. This approach was tested on 10% of the email file.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment version increased donor conversion by 75%! By simplifying the email and not making it feel like a templated, marketing email, people were more highly motivated to give. It removed all of the elements that distracted the reader from the message itself and clearly communicated what it was that we wanted them to do.

experiments.title: How asking people to pray instead of to give impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will asking people to pray before asking them to give inspire more generosity?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/17/2018 - 09/24/2018
During Hurricane Florence fundraising efforts, BGEA experimented with a direct ask vs. asking email subscribers to pray (before asking them to give a gift to support their Rapid Response Team’s ministry efforts after the wreckage was cleared).  This was the third email in a series of communications sent to the housefile. It had followed two prior emails that had made a direct solicitation of the file.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We observed a statistically significant lift in donor conversion of 30% by positioning the petition to pray prior to making an ask. After a visitor signed the pledge to pray, they were then presented with an opportunity to make a gift. 

This style of cultivation / solicitation tends to motivate a certain segment of donors. The previous two emails would have captured the people that were instantly motivated to make a gift and this was a way that allowed visitors to take a slower pathway to the donation form that first aligned them with the BGEA mission prior to making the ask. In future campaigns, this is a tactic that can be used in high urgency campaigns as well as on the website for organic website visitors.

It is also worth noting that the 30.9% increase in donor conversions also resulted in 42% more revenue given an additional increase in average gift.

experiments.title: How presenting an email acquisition opportunity as a forced pop-up impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase name acquisition on the Answers pages through a forced and exit-intent pop-up.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/01/2019 - 06/01/2019
Billy Graham Evangelical Association gets significant traffic to their Answers pages. At the bottom of these pages there is an opportunity for people to sign up to receive the BGEA newsletter. We hypothesized that presenting that acquisition opportunity in a different way might increase conversion and name acquisition for the organization. To test this, we developed a pop-up/exit-intent pop-up that appeared on desktop (and a mobile slide-up). Knowing that a lot of the people coming to the Answers pages were returning visitors, and that a forced pop-up on a page can cause a poor user experience, we delayed the pop-up from appearing until after a person had been on the page for 30 seconds. If they exited prior to that time and seeing the pop-up, we would then trigger an exit-intent pop-up. In addition to this, we also put a rule on the pop-ups to only show every third visit.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new method for presenting the acquisition opportunity increased conversion by 93.3%! The newsletter sign-up on the bottom of the page was getting lost with all of the other content on the page. By pulling that offer up from the bottom and presenting it when people were more highly engaged with the site, we were able to increase the motivation of people on the page to get the offer. In addition, the trigger parameters that were put on the pop-up created a more positive user experience and will allow the pop-up to be effective longer than just using a forced pop-up with no rules in place.
In our next phase of testing, we would like to test the kind of offer in the pop-up to see if we can increase the relevancy and appeal of what is being offered to people on these high-trafficked, highly-engaged pages.

experiments.title: How validating shared beliefs impacts name conversion on a pledge
experiments.researchQuestion: Would name acquisition increase if people had to validate their beliefs?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/05/2019 - 03/24/2019
Billy Graham Evangelistic Association has a significant amount of traffic going to a page called, “I am Not Ashamed.” On this page, they ask Christians to sign the proclamation that they are not ashamed of the Gospel. They use Scripture to reinforce this statement, a message from the CEO and president, and a form for people to fill out to sign the pledge. In an effort to increase name conversion through this pledge, we hypothesized adding check boxes to the belief statement. Before signing the pledge, people would then have to physically check the boxes affirming that they believed in the proclamation.
experiments.keyLearnings: By asking the reader to confirm their shared values—that they "believe the Bible is without error or fault in all its teaching, believe the Gospel brings salvation to everyone who believes, and believe as followers of Christ we much stand for the Gospel and not be ashamed of it", we were able to increase name acquisition (proclamation signers) by 11.3%. Having a reader physically check the boxes of the beliefs that they hold validated and reinforced those beliefs causing them to be more highly motivated to sign the pledge.

experiments.title: How communicating the impact of a recurring gift can increase conversion with new visitors
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase recurring giving by  communicating the impact of a recurring gift at the point a person decides on the kind of gift to give?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/01/2019 - 11/01/2019
On the BGEA primary donation page, we hypothesized that adding a line of copy communicating the impact of a recurring gift at the point of a person’s gift decision may influence a person’s decision to change their one-time gift to a recurring gift. No other elements were changed to the giving process.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found is that the additional language on the gift type had no impact overall. But when we started looking into the different kind of traffic, the story was much different. For new donors to the BGEA website, this additional value proposition language increased recurring giving by 48%! At the same time, the added copy actually caused a decrease in recurring giving for returning visitors. This is a fascinating discovery in understanding the different motivations of site visitors. New people to the site needed that extra reminder and reinforcement that their gift could go so much further.

experiments.title: How hiding gift designations on a primary donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will hiding the 14 gift designations increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/03/2019 - 05/26/2019
On BGEA’s primary donation page, they present 14 different designations for people to choose from when making a gift. In looking at this we hypothesized that the significant array of options for people to choose from may be causing friction in the donation pathway. A person would need to look through all of the 14 giving options and select where they’d like to designate their gift towards before moving on to the next step in the donation process. The test our hypothesis, we hid all 14 designations on the donation page and added a link that people could click on if they wanted to see all of the options.
experiments.keyLearnings: To our surprise, hiding the gift designations decreased donor conversion. This result was true for every device type and user segment (new vs. returning). While we saw a negative impact on overall donor conversion, we hypothesize that we may have seen an increase in "where most needed" but a decrease from the other designations. We are dissecting the data now to look into this hypothesis. We also hypothesize that by eliminating all gift designations, we may have removed some of the most used designations resulting in the decrease in conversion. We will now test into this hypothesis in two ways: hiding most of the designations except for the top 3-5, and then also testing a "most popular" designation by visually setting this off and adding value proposition to it.

experiments.title: How increasing perceived security on a primary donation page for desktop devices can impact donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding visual elements increase the perception of form security increase donor conversion on desktop devices?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/26/2019 - 03/27/2019
One of the most common reasons web visitors don’t complete transactions online is a perceived lack of security. Knowing this, Billy Graham Evangelical Association wondered if their primary donation pathway was losing potential donors because it didn’t visually represent how secure it was. They created a special security box around the credit card section of form and added a padlock into the shaded box to represent that the form was secure. This didn’t make the form more secure—it was already PCI compliant and had the highest levels of security. All these design tweaks did was increase perception of security with the end user. They launched an A/B test to see if it increased donor conversion as they hypothesized.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Adding visual elements to increase the perception of security on the donation form increased donor conversion by 20% on desktop devices.
This confirms our hypothesis that there is a segment of donors who experience anxiety when making a donation around the issue of security. The tactic that we tested helped to calm their fear, reduce friction through the donation pathway, and ultimately convert more people into donors.
This 20% increase in donor conversion has a significant impact on revenue for the organization because 67% of the overall revenue coming in from BGEA's primary donation page is from desktop devices.
What is interesting is that this same exact experiment on mobile/tablet saw a 16% decrease in donor conversion.
Because of this, we recommend rolling out the perceived security tactic on desktop only.

experiments.title: How decision friction impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would we be able to increase donor conversion by removing some of the gift designations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/28/2019 - 07/28/2019
On BGEA’s primary donation page, they offer people 14 different gift designations to choose from during the first step in their donation process. In an effort to optimize the giving experience and increase donor conversion, we previously tested removing all of the gift designation hypothesized narrowing down the number of gift designation options to the top five. Using historical giving data, we pulled the top five designations and developed a treatment that featured just those five. People would still be able to see the other 9 options if they wanted by clicking the “See more>>” link. We also developed another variation of the treatment that emphasized the top giving option as the “Most Urgent” need and removed the imagery and copy from the rest of the gift designations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for a month, we were able to increase overall donor conversion by 11.4% by narrowing down the gift designation options down from 14 to 5. Since this experiment was run on the primary donation page where motivations and audience can vary greatly, we analyzed all devices (mobile and desktop) and user types (new and returning). Regardless of device or user, donor conversion increased with the first treatment. The second treatment had differing results based on device and audience, but none of those results had high validity.
This experiment tells us that there was decision friction on the page in the number of designations and options we were presenting people. By reducing the numbers of options, we were able to increase donor conversion and overall revenue for the organization.
This page gets a significant volume of traffic to it. The potential impact that this experiment can have on revenue moving forward is substantial. In fact, based on historical giving, when the winning treatment is rolled out to 100% of the traffic over the next three months, it has the potential to bring in an additional projected $50k in revenue!

rows.name: Free "I'm Not a Liberal" Bumper Sticker

experiments.researchQuestion: Which email acquisition offer will lead to the greatest number of donors acquired on the follow on donation page?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/05/2013
As part of a email acquisition campaign, the Media Research Center ran visitors through a six question survey. The goal was to educate the visitors on the problems associated with the liberal media.  At the end, they were offered a premium in exchange for their email address.  After a successful name conversion, they were then led to a contextualized donation opportunity.
The quizzes had proven to be a good engagement tactic in past campaign but the question remained for what would make the best premium offer to get visitors to give both their name and, eventually, a gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The elements of this experiment dealt with the best method to capture email addresses.  However, the long term goal of the email capture was the eventual conversion to donors.
What we discovered is that the bumper sticker offer (which asked for a home address on the email capture page) actually drove a higher motivated audience to the donation page.  Despite lowering the click through traffic volume by 25% compared to the other treatments, by getting their address on the previous page and giving them a tangible premium, we made the follow-on donation ask more appealing.  As a result, we experienced a 250% increase in conversion rate.

experiments.title: How page layout can affect visitor motivation
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a single column design increase the number of petition signatures compared to a double column design?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/11/2015 - 08/13/2015
The Media Research Center’s mission is to ensure that the media does not try to shape public opinion through biased reporting. When the first video was released documenting the sale of fetal tissue by Planned Parenthood, the coverage of the video by the major news networks was mere seconds. In order to shed light on this bias by omission, the MRC started a petition to rally public support.
The original landing page for this petition was a two column design with the video and information about it on the left and the petition on the right. Since traffic was being driving by both social media and organic website visitors, we thought it might be better to rearrange the content so that the visitors were first presented with the information before being asked to sign the petition. As a result, we created a single column treatment to test against the control.
experiments.keyLearnings: The single column treatment ended up decreasing the conversion rate of the page by 16.5%. What we learned is that this audience was already educated on the topic and did not need the additional value proposition language before taking our desired action. With the two column layout, those wanting to sign the petition were able to do so right way. The single column layout didn’t let us take advantage of the high visitor motivation.

As part of an email acquisition campaign, Media Research Center visitors were offered a free bumper sticker in exchange for their name, email, and home address.  They were then taken to a landing page with a contextualized giving opportunity.  This page had historically produced a 0.8% immediate conversion rate.
We had recently completed an experiment that improved the general donation form conversion rate to 1.6%.  With this being higher, we wanted to see if the newly validated general donation page could convert better than the existing contextualized page.
The new treatment did not mention the petition they have just signed but did include language about a matching gift opportunity and emphasized 3rd party credibility indicators, such as quotes from well known figures, as a way to lower the anxiety associated with giving a gift online.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new treatment outperformed the contextualized donation page by 230%.  This proved the incentive behind the matching gift language and increased credibility outweighed the specificity of associating the gift with their previous action.
This learning led to ideas for additional tests such as including this language and imagery on other contextualized pages to help lower anxiety while maintaining the specificity that comes from a contextualized donation ask.

As part of a email acquisition campaign, the Media Research Center ran visitors through a six question survey. The goal was to educate the visitors on the problems associated with the liberal media.  At the end, they were offered a premium in exchange for their email address.  After a successful name conversion, they were then led to a contextualized donation opportunity.
The quizzes had proven to be a good engagement tactic in past campaign but the question remained for what would make the best premium offer to get visitors to give both their name and, eventually, a gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The petition versus the free PDF had no statististically significant difference in their conversions. However, the bumper sticker offer which required a home address, decreased the number of names acquired by 25%. In this situation, the added incentive of an tangible premium (the bumper sticker) was not of high enough value to overcome the friction that comes with asking for the home address.

However, the subsequent results seen on the donation page made the decrease in emails acquired an acceptable tradeoff.

In an effort to increase the volume of people who open the emails being sent, we hypothesized that an email sent from a person rather than the organization might result in higher open rates. To test this, we developed a treatment email that was sent from Jacinta Tegman. The sender was the only variable that was different for the email; all other email content remained consistent with the control.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By sending emails from a person rather than an organization, we were able to increase open rates by 38%. People are more motivated to engage with people, not organizations. When a person sees that an email is from an individual, it automatically feels authentic and personal. Emails from organizations usually signify it is a marketing email, or that the organization is wanting something. This leads to lower levels of engagement.
Getting more people to open the email is just the first step in the conversion process. The more people we are able to get to open the email, the more we are able to get to read the content and potentially convert and give. This personal approach in email is a method to continue to test all the way through the email content to see the full impact that it can have on donor conversion.

In reviewing historical promotional emails for the Global Gift Guide, it was clear that the organization has always leaned upon well designed and graphic-heavy images to promote the gift catalog. We wanted to experiment with emails in 2018 to verify if this approach would also work for World Concern.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 98% confidence score, this experiment validated that "plain text" style emails improved open rates by 35% for World Concern.
Technologically speaking, highly stylized and image-heavy emails are typically "caught" by the email server that receives them before sending them to the intended recipient, and have a higher chance of being codified as a spam message. This plain text style template not only has shown to improve engagement rates because people give to people, not organizations, it also typically yields much higher open and click volumes due to the higher inboxing rates.
When possible, always find a way to send message from a person at your organization to your donors.

experiments.title: Does a high urgency campaign with a direct ask and goal drive more results than general Christmas gift messaging?
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a high urgency campaign with a direct ask and goal drive more results than general Christmas gift messaging?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2018 - 12/20/2018
World Concern’s Global Gift Guide email strategy has traditionally focused upon messaging donors leading up to Christmas in a very transactional way.
As a part of the 2018 strategy, to test into creating a “High Urgency Campaign” that pulled out 4 of the most popular gifts (with the highest appeal and organizational mission alignment) and created a fundraising goal, with challenge messaging to challenge the donor to help us deliver specific gifts by a deadline.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The high urgency campaign messaging treatment essentially doubled the donor conversion rate (+99.4% increase) when compared to the transactional gift messages that had been traditionally used leading up to Christmas giving deadlines.
Typically, revenue lift experiments require far more than 75 transactions to validate, but we did also achieve a statistical significance in increasing revenue for the campaign through donations received through the treatment vs. the control. With a 96.5% level of confidence, we also saw an increase in the revenue per visitor statistic by 71.3%.
We would recommend further testing (and gathering far more than 75 transactions) to have full confidence with the revenue lift aspect of this type of experiment, but when combining both revenue lift potential with twice as many gifts, leveraging high urgency campaigns over transactional messages within the confines of a holiday gift catalog program appears to drive a dramatic improvement.

experiments.title: How a contextual value proposition and a reordered form affects donor conversion

Ended On: 07/09/2021

The donation pages also had copy on the left side of the page under an image, and the donation form flowing down the right side of the page.

First, they crafted new copy that thanked the user for completing the survey and presented a value proposition asking for a monthly donation. Second, they moved the donation form below the copy, so that the user was forced to read through the copy and absorb the value proposition before engaging with the form. The hypothesis was that the user would be more motivated by the contextual ask and that the ordering of the form would place the giving opportunity when the donor was primed to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new form and copy produced a 258% lift in donations. This shows that the contextual copy, combined with the reordered form, produced a much more likely donation outcome.
This provides many future implications for Scouting Gives and the appeal creation process, as well as simple form adjustments that can reduce friction and increase conversion.

experiments.title: How a one-column format donation page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reformatting the existing two-column donation form page layout to a single column for potential donors will reduce cognitive friction, increasing focus and improving donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/03/2022 - 03/26/2022
Upon reviewing the existing Boy Scouts of America donation page layout and flow, we recognized an opportunity to conduct an experiment based on previous NextAfter experiments and learnings. Based on this library of experiments, we made several significant changes to the layout of this critical donation page.
We first removed the imagery to elevate the communication priority of the reinforcing copy on the page. Next, we refined the layout from a two-column layout to a single-column format, introducing a more linear flow we believe reduces cognitive load that leads to a higher percentage of donors completing the form and making a donation. We also emphasized the perceived security of this form by boxing the payment information fields and reinforcing trust with the use of a lock icon, a web standard that most online shoppers today are familiar with.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The redesigned single-column donation page layout produced a 126% lift in donor conversion rate. This experiment shows us that the single-column form layout reduces user friction, introducing a more linear user journey that produced a much more likely positive donation outcome.
This provides many future opportunities for experimentation for BSA, demonstrating conclusively that relatively simple to execute form optimizations can measurably reduce friction and increase donor conversion.

experiments.title: Does Increasing the Number of Appeal Links Boost Email Clicks?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the # of donation appeal links for a campaign appeal email will achieve a higher number of clicks to the donation page because the additional links will reduce friction and capture reader attention at different moments of engagement within the email.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/11/2026 - 02/28/2026
Bible League Canada (BLC) is a ministry organization dedicated to placing Bibles and biblical resources into the hands of waiting believers in Canada and around the world. During one of their fundraising campaigns, we wanted to test whether increasing the number of appeal links in an email would help drive more visitors to the donation page.
We decided to test this in Email #2 of this campaign, which served as a key follow-up touchpoint. While the first email introduced the need, Email #2 was designed to reinforce urgency and convert engaged readers into donors. Because it targeted an already-warmed audience, it represented a meaningful opportunity to optimize click-through behavior and drive additional gifts.
This experiment asked: Would increasing the number of donation links within the body of the email increase clicks to the donation page? Or is a single plain-text link sufficient once donor motivation is established?
The Control version included one plain-text donation link placed within the email.
The Treatment version included the same plain-text donation link, but also hyperlinked two additional portions of body copy higher up in the email. These additional links directed to the same donation page and provided readers with more opportunities to click as they engaged with the message.
The language, story, and overall appeal copy did not change between versions. The only variable tested was the number and placement of donation links within the email.
We randomly split the Email #2 audience between the two versions and measured clicks to the donation page as the primary outcome. Clicks on non-donation links (such as social media icons or the organization logo) were excluded from our analysis. Click results were pulled from February 11 – 28.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of this test confirmed our hypothesis that increasing the number of appeal links within the email would cause a lift in clicks to the donation page.
The Control email generated 47 clicks, resulting in a 1.8% click-through rate. The Treatment generated 90 clicks, resulting in a 3.5% click-through rate. This means that adding additional appeal links in the email drove a 90.9% increase in clicks to the donation page, with a statistically significant 90.9% level of confidence.
When readers feel motivated to act, even briefly, having an immediate opportunity to click increases the likelihood of follow-through. The additional links likely reduced friction by allowing readers to click at the moment conviction or urgency peaked — instead of requiring them to scroll down to find the link.
Due to the donation sample size from this test, we were not able to validate any specific downstream impacts, such as differences in total gifts or revenue between versions. As a result, this test confirms increased giving intent (clicks), but does not yet confirm increased giving outcomes.
Further testing is needed to determine if this lift in clicks consistently translates to increased gifts and revenue, and also to confirm whether this effect holds across other campaign emails like a launch email or final day ask.
Our recommended next step is to test this hypothesis again across multiple emails within a fundraising campaign and evaluate not only clicks, but downstream conversion and revenue impact. Aggregating results across several sends will help determine whether this tactic should become a standard structural best practice for Bible League Canada’s email program.

experiments.title: Will Testing Different CTAs in Facebook Ad Copy to Increase Donor Conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a direct ask like “Click to Give Bibles” for Facebook audiences will achieve higher donor conversion than a softer “Click to Learn More” call-to-action because because clearer donation language reduces ambiguity and increases motivation to act on the giving opportunity.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/08/2026 - 01/30/2026
Bible League Canada was preparing to launch a new set of Facebook ads focused on a clear value proposition: asking users to donate to place Bibles with waiting believers around the world. In previous campaigns, some ad variations ended with “Click to Learn More >>”, while others used a more direct ask of “Click to Give Bibles >>”.
To better understand which type of call-to-action language drives higher donor conversions, the team set up a simple A/B test. The only element changed between versions was the final line of the primary ad copy:
Control: “Click to Learn More >>”
Treatment: “Click to Give Bibles >>”
Everything else — the ad creative, targeting, and budget — remained the same. The ads were shown to a broad Facebook audience, including existing donors, subscribers, lookalike audiences, and open targeting.
The big question we wanted to answer was: Does a direct ask improve donor response by providing clarity and urgency, or does a softer, curiosity-based CTA encourage more engagement that leads to giving?
experiments.keyLearnings:
We ran this test for 23 days, and found that the direct, action-oriented call-to-action language significantly outperformed the softer alternative in driving donations through Facebook. Ads that ended with “Click to Give Bibles” led to 117.6% more donations than those ending with “Click to Learn More,” with a statistically significant 98.6% level of confidence. This is a major win for Bible League Canada and strongly supports using clearer giving language in our future ads.

When we dug further into the data, we found that this stronger performance was driven by both increased engagement and improved conversion:
The direct CTA generated 5.5% more ad clicks compared to the control, trending toward statistical validity at 92.7% confidence.
 	Those who clicked the Treatment were also twice as likely to convert into donors, with a 1.75% instant conversion rate for the Treatment compared to 0.88% for the Control.
the Treatment also showed a higher average gift

Gross Cost Per Donor dropped from $170 (Control) to $87 (Treatment)
 	ROI more than doubled, from 0.25 (Control) to 0.61 (Treatment)

experiments.title: How removing the organization’s name from an email sender impacts engagement during a high urgency campaign

rows.name: From: Jody at Bible League Canada

experiments.researchQuestion: We hypothesized that removing” at Bible League Canada” from the email sender name would improve the open rate of their emails. This would make the email look and feel more like a one-to-one communication in the inbox instead of an organization’s “marketing” email.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/19/2024 - 04/29/2024
Bible League Canada (BLC) is a ministry organization that uses members of its staff as email senders. The sender name for these staff emails always includes the person’s name and” at Bible League Canada” after it. The content of BLC’s emails feels very authentic and has helped foster strong relationships with their email list subscribers. Still, we wanted to collect data to understand whether including their organization’s name in their email sender name was hurting their email open rates.
We decided to test this concept during one of BLC’s high-urgency campaigns. We tested it with a direct appeal email sent from Hannah, one of Bible League Canada’s main email senders. The results of that test can be found here.
We wanted to retest this hypothesis using another sender during the campaign to see if the positive results we discovered would be limited to the sender we chose.
We ran this as an A/B email test, with 50% of BLC’s housefile list receiving the control email and the other 50% receiving the treatment email. The email’s subject line and content were the same for both variants; the only variable changed was the email sender’s name. These were the variants in this test:
Control Email – From name was “Jody at Bible League Canada”
Treatment Email – From name was “Jody”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing the organization's name from the email sender's name can significantly increase open rates. The treatment email, with the sender name "Jody," saw a 40.8% increase in opens compared to the control email, "Jody at Bible League Canada." This result was statistically significant at a 100.0% confidence level, indicating a strong impact.
We could not validate an increase in clicks from the treatment version. With this second test result, Bible League Canada's audience appears to respond well when appeals are sent without the "at Bible League Canada" postscript. This test should be revisited in the future to see if it is a "fad" or something that remains constant for their email file. But for now, using personal, authentic sender names should be the template for email cultivation and activation.

experiments.title: How an adding a relevant image on a petition landing page impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Adding the same relevant image to a petition landing page that people saw on the social media ad will increase the email acquisition rate because the image provides continuity for people.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/21/2025 - 05/29/2025
Mountain States Legal Foundation protects property rights and economic liberty, defends the right to keep and bear arms, champions free speech and association, and upholds the principle of equal protection.
We ran this test on an instant donation page from a petition. Audience: People from social media ads.
We wanted to know if more people will donate because people see an image on the ad and then the same image on the landing page. We thought this would provide continuity between the channels.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding the same relevant image to a petition landing page that people saw on the social media ad did not lead to an increase in email acquisition rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a significant decrease in transactions and recurring donations compared to the control group.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the impact of image continuity between different channels. While the hypothesis was based on the idea that continuity could enhance the user experience and engagement, the results indicate that this may not always be the case.

Moving forward, it is crucial to test hypotheses thoroughly before implementing them on a larger scale. This can help avoid potential negative impacts on donation rates or other key metrics. Additionally, it is important to analyze the results objectively and be prepared to pivot strategies based on data-driven insights, even if they contradict initial assumptions.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of experimentation and data analysis in optimizing donation pages and marketing strategies. It is essential to continuously learn from both successful and unsuccessful experiments to improve future campaigns and drive better outcomes for the nonprofit organization.

experiments.title: How using a content offer instead of a direct ask in an article interstitial affects click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a free ebook for website visitors will achieve an increase in click-through rate because it provides immediate value to the audience, aligns with their interests, and incentivizes engagement by presenting a tangible benefit in exchange for their attention, making the offer more appealing and actionable.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/08/2025 - 01/08/2025
MPR has direct ask interstitials on its article pages throughout its website. They wanted to see how a content offer would affect click-through rate on the interstitial so they tested advertising their Talking Sense ebook for 50% of traffic to see.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment:

1. Offering a free ebook as a content offer can significantly increase the click-through rate on interstitials.

2. The treatment group, which received the ebook offer, experienced a 93.7% increase in clicks compared to the control group.

3. The results were statistically significant with a 100.0% confidence level, indicating that the ebook offer had a clear impact on user behavior.

**It's important to note that the test did not track email acquisition or donor conversion so we do not know the impact on those two metrics.

How to use these learnings in future experimentation:

1. Consider using content offers, such as ebooks, as incentives to encourage user engagement and click-throughs.

2. Continue to test different types of content offers to see which ones resonate best with the audience.

3. Use A/B testing and statistical significance to measure the effectiveness of different strategies and make data-driven decisions for future campaigns.

4. Incorporate learnings from this experiment into overall marketing and engagement strategies to optimize user interactions and drive desired outcomes.

The page already highlighted the number of people that would be reached with a donor’s gift. However, we hypothesized that allowing the donors to drag a slider that dynamically changes the number of people reached would increase the clarity of the offer when compared with the current experience.
We used VWO to create a graphical, interactive slider on the page that dynamically changed the gift amount and the corresponding number of people the gift would reach. We split traffic 50/50, and all campaign sources were directed to the test.

experiments.title: How a pop-up inviting one-time donors to convert their donation to recurring impacts recurring donor conversion on donation pages
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a popup inviting one-time donors to convert their one-time donation to a recurring gift will have a more significant impact and will increase recurring giving by making the value proposition of a recurring gift more appealing and transparent to the donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/22/2024 - 07/31/2024
This organization uses a tabbed donation form to enable donors to select either a one-time or a recurring donation. Last year, they tested adding a callout to the recurring tab to highlight the greater impact of becoming a recurring donor. This validated an increase in recurring giving and became a part of all their donation pages.
We hypothesized that we could further increase recurring giving on the page by firing a popup when someone clicks the donate button on the form. This popup would invite donors to give a smaller recurring gift to have a bigger impact than the higher one-time donation they had selected. The form would automatically ask for a gift that was 75% of the value of the one-time donation originally chosen.
We ran this experiment on instant donation pages and the donation pages of their one-time donor-focused July Campaign. Traffic was split 50/50.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The popup inviting one-time donors to convert their donation to a recurring gift significantly increased recurring donations by 129.4% for all traffic with a high level of statistical confidence (99.6%). This indicates that emphasizing the value proposition of recurring donations can effectively incentivize donors to choose ongoing giving.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue exploring ways to highlight the benefits of recurring donations and nudge donors toward choosing this option. Additionally, leveraging popups strategically to interrupt the donation process and present compelling offers can be a powerful tool in increasing donor engagement and conversion rates.

Most importantly, we found that the popup had no statistically significant impact on overall donor conversion, so we were not sacrificing one-time donations by running the popup. Because of the significantly higher retention rate and lifetime value of recurring donors, this increase in recurring giving could have significant impacts on donor revenue for this organization.

experiments.title: How a dear reader appeal for article readers impacts donor conversion outside of a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a dear reader for the article readers will achieve greater donor conversion because it will increase the appeal, clarity, and exclusivity of making a gift to CBN News because it will increase the appeal, clarity, and exclusivity of making a gift to CBN News.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/26/2023 - 02/18/2023
The Christian Broadcasting Network is a Christian news and media ministry that reaches millions of people around the world and provides humanitarian aid and practical resources to those in need. For their December Campaign, they tested a dear reader appeal to see if it would improve donor conversion on their article pages. While we weren’t able to validate a lift in conversion during the experiment, we had a directional lift in revenue which we hypothesized was due to the additional value proposition readers received from the dear reader message.
We wanted to retest this approach outside of the influences of a high-urgency campaign. We decided to keep the same value proposition from the two variants we tested in December and add a 3rd variation that would reference how fewer than 1% of readers give to keep CBN online and on-air.
Dear Reader #1 would emphasize CBN’s messaging and worldview. “CBN News doesn’t bombard you with negative views, secular agendas, skewed statistics, or reporters’ political viewpoints. We’re here to tell you the truth, and to give you hope. And every CBN News story that you read and watch relies on the generosity of readers like you.”
Dear Reader #2 would emphasize CBN’s unique expertise. “Our award-winning journalists give you 24/7 breaking news, U.S. and international news, health, and Christian entertainment news. It’s reporting that you aren’t likely to see anywhere else—information you’ve relied on for many years! And the CBN News stories you read and watch depend on the generosity of readers like you.
Dear Reader #3 would emphasize CBN’s unique expertise AND how few readers give to keep their articles available. “You might be surprised to discover that less than 1% of our readers give to keep CBN News Available.”
They split the traffic four ways and ran the experiment for 20 days.  We also excluded any donations over $1,000 from the results to prevent them from skewing the experiment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Consistently during the experiment, the dear reader approach was winning for donor conversion. However, there was a surge in giving on mobile in particular the last week of the test that caused V3 with the 1% statistic to win out overall. We have now validated that a dear reader is a successful tactic to use during important giving seasons on CBN News' article pages and that language emphasizing the exclusive award-winning journalism of CBN news is highly motivational to donors. Citing how few readers give to keep these articles available also seems to motivate higher generosity.

For our next experiment, we will test a different placement of this messaging using a dynamic popup that becomes a sticky bar when scrolling to see if that placement improves conversion against the winning dear reader.

This doesn’t mean that they will definitely convert, but it does show that it slightly increases the likelihood of conversion.

experiments.title: How clarity in advertising copy affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a softer ask to “learn how” deliver more conversions than a direct ask to “get the eBook”?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2016 - 08/12/2016
The Stanford Graduate School of Business ran Twitter ads to offer their new eBook Driving Innovation and Ideas to the market. However, they were not sure about how motivated their audience was to download an eBook. They ran a test to determine if a softer ask could produce a higher clickthrough rate. The “Learn how” ad in this test produced a 43.2% increase in clickthroughs, which proved the hypothesis correct. However, they knew that all clicks are not created equal, and wanted to make sure that the clicks from the treatment ad were also producing a higher number of conversion.
They ran a second set of data to determine if the treatment ad also produced an increase in conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings: While the treatment ad produced a 43.2% lift in clickthrough rate, the “get the eBook” ad produced nearly 50% more conversions. This is a great reminder that “all clicks are not created equal”. Sometimes, traffic can be increased by lowering the clarity of the offer, which can produce less motivated traffic that is less likely to accept the offer. In this case, an increase in clickthrough rate does not outweigh the increase in conversion, making the direct ask the winner.

experiments.title: How increasing clarity in advertising copy affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How does increasing clarity in advertising copy affect conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/05/2016 - 05/19/2016
The Stanford Graduate School of Business was promoting their free eBook, Driving Innovation. They knew that the ad image on a Facebook ad was the most visible and impactful part of the ad. They wanted to test the impact of copy within the image, and how much specificity within that copy would increase conversion.
The control version of the ad used copy that highlighted the “desired effect” of the eBook: “Learn the secrets to driving innovation at work”. Since the rest of the copy reinforced this “desired effect”, they developed a treatment that was much more specific about the offer: “Get the new eBook from Stanford Business”.
They launched A/B ad test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment, with added specificity, resulted in a 52% increase in conversion. This shows us that while desired effect psychology might increase conversion on the landing page, at this stage of the funnel, we must be more specific about how the user is going to achieve that desired effect. In this case, they will achieve that by downloading an eBook.
Increasing clarity around the offer increased conversion, even when that “micro-yes” was early in the conversion funnel.

experiments.title: How “desired effect” psychology affects email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which psychological approach will convert the most visitors?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2015 - 01/25/2016
The Stanford Graduate School of Business launched a new ebook centered around developing a culture of innovation. They wanted to test different psychological approaches with the headline to see if one delivered a measurable lift over the others. They drafted four headlines:
1. “Driving Ideas and Innovation”: this simple headline tested the power of congruency, as it mirrored the title of the ebook.
2. “Get 10 must read articles from Stanford Business on driving innovation”: this headline was the only one that included the brand name, and told the reader what they would receive.
3. “Generate breakthrough ideas and create a culture of innovation”: this headline tested the power of the “desired effect” — not telling the reader what they would get immediately, but the result of the learnings
4. “Find out how you can develop a culture that drives growth and encourages innovation”: this headline teased the visitor with what they will learn, and assumed they were at the very top of the funnel in their decision-making process.
They launched a test that evenly distributed the four headlines between inbound traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings: The test proved that the “desired effect” headline produced an 8.2% lift over the control and the other variants, which tells us that when we tell people the end result of consuming the content, they are most incentivized to accept the offer.

experiments.title: How “Yes/No” Autonomy Language on a CYE Sticky Bar Impacts Conversion Rate

Overall, this experiment's results highlight the value of providing users with autonomy and decision-making opportunities in driving conversions. By understanding and leveraging this psychology, future experimentation efforts can focus on creating more engaging and compelling user experiences.

experiments.title: How an aligned, value-driven image of an ebook on a landing page impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using imagery that shows the content of an ebook for users visiting an ebook landing page will achieve an increase in email sign-ups because the imagery will showcase the value and ebook content more effectively.

During the Christmas season, Mission Aviation Fellowship (MAF) was running a devotional ebook acquisition offer. When running an offer like this, the landing page will typically feature a 3D mockup image of the ebook cover to help users on the page understand what they are receiving.
For this devotional offer, the ads on Facebook used a “scattered pages” design to showcase some of the unique offerings inside the ebook (devotions, Bible verses, crossword puzzles, etc.). We wondered if using this imagery on the devotional ebook landing page would help to enhance the value proposition and increase conversions.
We ran this as an A/B test for 29 days, with 50% of landing page traffic seeing the image of the 3D ebook, and the other 50% of traffic seeing the “scattered pages” design.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that utilizing imagery that shows the content of an ebook on a landing page can significantly increase email sign-ups. In this case, the treatment version, which featured an image showcasing some of the unique offerings inside the ebook, led to a 35.5% increase in emails acquired compared to the control version that only showed the 3D ebook cover. This lift was at a 99.2% LOC, which means that the result is considered statistically valid.

In future experiments, it is important to continue testing imagery on the landing page that effectively showcases the value and content of the content offer. This can help capture the attention of users and communicate the benefits of the ebook more clearly, ultimately leading to higher conversion rates.

Further testing should also be done to determine the impact of aligning the imagery on the landing page with any imagery that users are seeing at the front of the funnel through ads, emails, MMS, etc.

experiments.title: How calling out a monthly gift on a main donation page affects recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a monthly callout to the recurring option on the donation form for potential donors visiting the main donation page will achieve a higher recurring donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/22/2022 - 10/21/2022
Mission Aviation Fellowship wanted to test adding a “callout” to the recurring giving option on their main donation form to see if it would increase the number of recurring donations instead of one-time donations.
We added the callout to their main donation page for 50% of the traffic. The callout was a short, one-sentence description on how monthly giving has the greatest impact year-round.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment saw a 455% lift in monthly donor conversion with a 98.9% level of confidence on mobile devices specifically. We believe this happened because the callout absorbs a lot of white space on the mobile view making the recurring donation option stand out more to the user on this page compared to the control donation page or to the treatment page on a desktop.

Other notable takeaways that we saw in this experiment were a 33.9% lift in conversion rate for the all-traffic segment and a 28% lift in revenue for the all-traffic segment. Although neither one of those segments was validated, we believe the lift in conversion for both all-traffic segments can be attributed to the extra boost of value proposition that the donor was given when they were in the process of making their gift. Even though the callout was for monthly giving, it served as an extra piece of confirmation that a gift to Mission Aviation Fellowship was helping isolated people around the world. Therefore we believe the extra reminder within the process convinced more people to make a recurring gift and/or increase the size of their gift.

The last notable takeaway from this experiment is that this callout did not negatively affect donations altogether. We did not want to make users believe that a one-time gift was less impactful than a recurring gift, and it seems from the data, the callout did not have this impact. Donations for all giving only had 3.5% lift in conversion rate with a 34% level of confidence.

To continue further testing, we need to run an altered experiment for desktop users only.

Moving forward, it may be beneficial to further explore donor motivations and preferences through qualitative research or surveys. Understanding donors' perspectives and how they perceive different messages can provide valuable insights for designing effective fundraising strategies. experiments.title: How showing a recurring pop-up to users who are about to make a one-time donation affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a recurring pop-up to the instant donation page for petition signers will achieve a higher recurring donor conversion rate because the donor will be able to make more of an impact with a monthly gift at potentially a lower cost.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/28/2023 - 05/21/2023
MSLF wanted to increase its amount of recurring donors. To test into increasing recurring donors coming in from petitions, they decided to add a recurring pop-up on the donation page once someone hits “donate now” at the bottom of the giving form.
This recurring pop-up calculated the new recurring ask based on how much their one-time gift was:
It will check if the one-time amount they give is less than $1000 then take 60% of the original amount rounded to the nearest $5.
If that is less than $20 then it will set it to $20.
If it’s more than $100, then it will set it to $100.
After they ran this experiment for one month, we found that the pop-up increased recurring giving by .13% with a 84.1% level of confidence. But we also found that it decreased one-time giving by 37% with an 87% level of confidence.
Due to the average gift in that experiment being $26.89, we believed the calculations of the pop-up caused the recurring ask to not be much less or the same as their original one-time gift which deterred them from giving at all.
We decided to retest this recurring pop-up with different calculations to see if the results would change:
Anybody who gave $100 or less, ask for a recurring gift. If they gave more than $100, do not show the pop-up.
$20 – $100 = 60% of what their gift was
$20 or less = Just ask for a recurring gift of whatever their gift amount was
We believe these calculations will better reflect what a recurring gift would look like in comparison to what a donor would give one-time.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The recurring pop-up caused one one-time donor to convert their one-time gift to a monthly gift. There were no recurring donors from the control donation page.

Unfortunately, the recurring pop-up caused a 43.8% decrease in the overall conversion rate with a 96% level of confidence.

We believe this means that most people who were about to make a one-time donation left the page when presented with the option to turn their one-time gift into a recurring gift. The recurring pop-up added friction to the donation process.

MSLF decided to test this concept on their Idaho Stockwater petition campaign in Facebook. To test this, they had one campaign with a typical-looking Facebook ad and a second campaign with just an image that looked like an organic post.
Each campaign had the same budget and was targeting the same audiences in Facebook.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The organic post caused a decrease of 32.1% in click-through rate with a 99.9% level of confidence. Although our click-through rate decreased, the organic post positively affected most of the downstream metrics:

Spend - Control ($413, Treatment ($409)

CPM - Control ($40.20), Treatment ($11.82)

CPC - Control ($1.15), Treatment ($0.50)

% Sign Up - Control (69.7%), Treatment (76.7%)

Emails - Control (251), Treatment (628) - 10% increase in email conversion with a 98.6% LoC

ICR - Control (2.39%), Treatment (1.27%) - 41.4% decrease in donor conversion with a 63.8% LoC

Donors - Control (6), Treatment (8)

GCPN - Control ($1.65), Treatment ($0.65)

GCPD - Control ($69), Treatment ($51)

ROI - Control (0.33), Treatment (0.38)

Because traffic was so much cheaper to get at the Facebook ad level (CPM and CPC), we saw our downstream metrics perform better due to the volume of users going through the funnel. Although our ICR decreased by 41.4%, it did not negatively affect our GCPD which was $18 cheaper for the organic ads.

This small test produced a big lift in revenue, but there’s much more to be tested on this page—including the value proposition.

experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/28/2025 - 02/21/2025
In an effort to increase sign-up rates on MPR’s Talking Sense e-book landing page, we conducted a headline test designed to more effectively communicate the value of the offer to visitors. Our goal was to determine whether a more explicit, benefit-driven headline would lead to higher engagement and conversions.

experiments.keyLearnings:

This increase was statistically significant with a confidence level of 95.5%, indicating that the treatment version outperformed the control. Based on these results, we recommend rolling out the improved headline to all traffic.

These findings reinforce that MPR’s audience engages more when the benefits are clearly articulated.

Furthermore, the treatment also led to a 12.9% increase in donations at a 67.1% LOC. While this metric did not meet our standard of 95% validity, it does suggest that the treatment motivated more visitors to make donations over the control.

Building on this success, our next tests will explore how refining the CTA and button clarity can further enhance conversions.

experiments.title: How form location on a survey landing page impacts the email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that moving the form below the survey for MPR Listener Survey landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email conversion rate because participants will focus solely on completing the survey first without feeling pressured to provide personal information upfront, creating a more seamless and user-friendly experience.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/18/2025 - 03/20/2025
In an effort to boost the email acquisition rate on MPR’s Listener Survey landing page, we opted to test into relocating the form to the bottom of the page.
Our hypothesis was that this change would reduce the friction happening at the top of the page therefore increasing the number of people who would complete the survey.
Control: form above the survey
Treatment: form below the survey
experiments.keyLearnings:

The treatment version, where the form was placed at the bottom of the page, saw a 39.2% increase in Emails Acquired for All Traffic with a 99.1% statistical level of confidence, indicating that this change was highly effective in improving the user experience and increasing conversions and should be rolled out to all traffic.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the impact of page layout on user behavior. Placing the form below the survey reduced the friction at the top of the page, allowing participants to focus on completing the survey first without feeling pressured to provide personal information upfront. This created a more seamless and user-friendly experience ultimately leading to a higher conversion rate.

Moving forward, we should continue to test different page layout elements, such as form placement, language, and design, to optimize the user experience and improve conversion rates. It is also important to analyze the results of each experiment carefully and draw actionable insights to inform future optimization strategies.

experiments.title: Redesigning the Recurring Ask Module: Can a More Distinct and Motivating Post-Gift Offer Increase Monthly Donor Revenue?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making the recurring ask module more visually distinct, emotionally motivating, and easier to act on for recent one-time donors will increase revenue per visitor by prompting more monthly gifts or higher-value commitments, because reducing friction and reinforcing urgency and donor identity leverages post-gift momentum and encourages deeper investment.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/15/2025 - 11/12/2025
CitizenGO is a political advocacy organization that mobilizes nearly 20 million citizens around the world to take action in defense of life, family, and freedom. Their digital engagement model invites users to sign petitions aligned with their values, then make a one-time donation to support the movement. After donating, supporters land on a receipt page that includes an additional opportunity: to become a monthly donor and sustain the fight long-term.
In an effort to increase the rate of monthly donor conversion, we wanted to test into how we could acquire more monthly gifts on these receipt pages. And in our review of the current recurring gift ask on the receipt page, we identified some key areas of opportunity. The existing module blended into the page layout and offered three preset gift amounts plus a custom field, which may have created friction and choice overload. It also lacked strong motivational framing, focusing mainly on organizational independence.
Because recurring conversions on this page are typically low, the team recognized that minor copy or layout tweaks would likely be insufficient to produce a measurable change. Instead, we pursued a holistic redesign to increase both the visibility and motivational clarity of the recurring ask.
The Treatment featured a complete visual and copy refresh: a bright blue design to visually separate it from the receipt content, simplified choice architecture (one default gift amount with optional customization), and more emotionally compelling language. The new copy emphasized urgency, opposition, and donor identity—positioning monthly supporters as critical to winning ongoing battles. This design was based off of a well-performing recurring gift popup from a donation platform that CitizenGO is not using.
One important thing to note is that the Treatment did not use any higher gift amount than the Control. Instead, the default amount shown in the Treatment’s recurring ask matched the middle ask option from the Control experience. So if a user would have been asked on the Control for $7, $10, or $20 a month, the Treatment would just ask for $10/month.
This test ran in five languages—English, Spanish, French, German, and Italian—and, while expected to have low absolute conversion volume, aimed to determine whether a more distinct, motivating, and streamlined module could better convert new donors into committed monthly givers.
experiments.keyLearnings:
1. Change in Recurring Gifts
Across all languages, the Treatment increased the number of recurring gifts by 17%, but with a confidence level of 82%. While this does not meet the 95% threshold for statistical significance, it represents a strong directional lift, especially given the relatively low sample size.
No individual language segment reached statistical confidence, suggesting that more traffic is needed to validate conversion differences at the segment level.
2. Average Gift Amount
Donors who gave monthly in the Treatment variant contributed higher average gift amounts, rising from €13.93 in the Control to €16.83 in the Treatment. This lift contributed meaningfully to the overall revenue gains and suggests that the redesigned module not only encouraged giving, but also positioned the monthly commitment in a way that inspired greater generosity.
3. Revenue Per Visitor
This was the most compelling outcome of the test.
•	Among the EN (UK) audience, the Treatment drove a 67% lift in revenue per visitor (€0.78 → €1.30) with 98% statistical confidence.
•	The Italian segment saw an 83% lift in revenue per visitor (€0.73 → €1.35), with 93% confidence—just shy of statistical significance, but strongly directional.
•	Most importantly, in aggregate across all languages, the Treatment drove a 39.5% increase in revenue per visitor (from €0.66 to €0.91), with 98.3% statistical confidence.
🎉 This is a major win for CitizenGO, showing that the new module significantly increased the value of post-donation traffic. The combination of higher conversion volume (directionally) and higher gift size resulted in meaningful revenue growth.
⸻
This test shows us that a thoughtfully designed, psychologically grounded upsell experience can convert new donors into higher-value monthly supporters—especially when the ask is timely, low-friction, and tied to the donor’s identity and urgency.
For future testing, we can exlore more angles of personalization, value proposition, or increased ask amounts to drive more engagement and revenue.

experiments.title: How copy and checkbox design drive GDPR consent opt-ins during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the GDPR consent process by using a single checkbox with clear privacy language will reduce friction for users and increase the opt-in rate for receiving future communications.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/17/2025 - 04/24/2025
CitizenGO is a global community that uses online petitions and action alerts to defend life, family, and liberty. Their platform enables individuals to influence decision-makers worldwide by leveraging the collective support of like-minded citizens.
This experiment stems from a review of GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation) requirements, which mandate that organizations obtain explicit consent from users before collecting or processing their personal data, including email addresses. As part of this compliance, it’s important to design petition sign-up forms in a way that ensures users are properly informed and opt in to receive future communications.
For this test, we are evaluating different designs for the GDPR consent checkbox on petition landing pages. The current design includes the following two options:
I want to know if this petition wins and how I can help other citizen petitions.
I don’t want to know how this petition or other important petitions are progressing.
The proposed test will compare three variations of the GDPR consent checkbox:
Control – The current checkbox design to track opt-ins.
Treatment 1 – A simplified design with a single checkbox and long privacy copy above it, providing detailed information in accordance with GDPR requirements.
Treatment 2 – A “Christian Lawyer” style design featuring a concise statement next to the checkbox, with a link to additional privacy policy details placed below the petition submission button.
The test will be run on the German petition pages (launching inline with their Stop Digital Euro Campaign) with traffic split evenly across all three variations. This experiment will help determine which design leads to higher consent opt-in rates while maintaining GDPR compliance.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test ran for 1 week and we observed significant differences in opt-in clicks across the different treatments. This result is parallel to what we saw when we ran this test on Hazteoir petition pages.

Results:
Control: 14,099 samples | 535 opt-in clicks | 6,707 email acquisitions
 	Treatment 1: 14,274 samples | 1,272 opt-in clicks | 6,667 email acquisitions
 	Treatment 2: 14,155 samples | 1,680 opt-in clicks | 6,808 email acquisitions
137.7%
213.7% increase
100% Level of Confidence (LoC)
Control conversion rate: ~47.6%
 	Treatment 1 conversion rate: ~46.7%
 	Treatment 2 conversion rate: ~48.1%
Recommendation:
Treatment 2
