experiments.title: How copy and checkbox design drive GDPR consent opt-ins
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the GDPR consent process by using a single checkbox with clear privacy language will reduce friction for users and increase the opt-in rate for receiving future communications.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/05/2025 - 03/12/2025
This experiment stems from a review of GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation) requirements, which mandate that organizations obtain explicit consent from users before collecting or processing their personal data, including email addresses. As part of this compliance, it’s important to design petition sign-up forms in a way that ensures users are properly informed and opt in to receive future communications.
For this test, we are evaluating different designs for the GDPR consent checkbox on petition landing pages. The current design includes the following two options:
I want to know if this petition wins and how I can help other citizen petitions.
I don’t want to know how this petition or other important petitions are progressing.
The proposed test will compare three variations of the GDPR consent checkbox:
Control – The current checkbox design to track opt-ins.
Treatment 1 – A simplified design with a single checkbox and long privacy copy above it, providing detailed information in accordance with GDPR requirements.
Treatment 2 – A design featuring a concise statement next to the checkbox, with a link to additional privacy policy details placed below the petition submission button.
The test will be run with traffic split evenly across all three variations. This experiment will help determine which design leads to higher consent opt-in rates while maintaining GDPR compliance.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test ran for 1 week, and we observed significant differences in opt-in clicks across the different treatments.

 	Control: 10,980 samples, 481 opt-in clicks, 5,599 email acquisitions
 	Treatment 1 (Long): 11,262 samples, 1,094 opt-in clicks, 5,718 email acquisitions
 	Treatment 2 (Christian): 11,195 samples, 1,387 opt-in clicks, 5,762 email acquisitions

The Treatment 1 (Long) increased opt-in clicks by 121.7% at a 100% Level of Confidence (LoC), while Treatment 2 (Christian) showed an even stronger increase of 182.8% at a 100% LoC. Both treatments outperformed the control, where the opt-in clicks were much lower.

Despite these significant increases in opt-in clicks, there was no impact on email acquisitions. This is fine, as the goal of the test was to increase consent to the terms (via checking the box) rather than directly driving email sign-ups. The conversion rates for email acquisitions were as follows:

 	Control: ~4.38%
 	Treatment 1 (Long): ~4.13%
 	Treatment 2 (Christian): ~4.17%

This indicates that while the treatments successfully increased opt-in clicks, they did not lead to a notable change in email sign-ups. Given the strong results from Treatment 2 (Christian), we recommend implementing this treatment across all petition pages. This approach had the highest increase in opt-in clicks and aligns with the goal of improving user engagement with the petition.

experiments.title: How a popup prompting homepage visitors who abandon the donation page during a matching challenge to complete their gift impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a popup reminding visitors who abandon the donation page to complete their gift to double their impact will increase donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/26/2022 - 01/23/2023
The Christian Broadcasting Network is a Christian news and media ministry that reaches millions of people around the world and provides humanitarian aid and practical resources to those in need. During their Giving Tuesday campaign, they tested a popup that would display for visitors to their homepage who started the process of making a gift but then abandoned the donation page before it was completed.
That experiment found that it increased donor revenue. They wanted to also see the impact it had on donations and, because of how the experiment had been set up, we weren’t able to determine the impact it had on donor conversion. So, we retested this popup during the last days of their December campaign to see the impact on donor conversion of this popup.
Because this popup only fires for those who abandon the donation page without making a gift, the control audience in this experiment shows only one sample in the results. Because the control audience, those who abandon the page but don’t get a popup, aren’t recorded in these results. Rather, we are looking to see the conversion of presenting that popup to abandons, so how many donors can we draw back to the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In all, 53 people who abandoned the donation page and saw the popup clicked to return to the page. Of these, 26% completed their gift. So, the popup successfully brought back 14 donors who otherwise would have been lost and with them approximately $5K in additional revenue.

This tactic is one that we will continue to test with other value propositions. So, for example, would we reactivate more abandoned donors by presenting them with a lower appeal than what they were considering making? Or would we reactivate more by offering them an eBook or pledge that then pointed to a donation page? These are questions we will seek to answer in future testing.

experiments.title: How a slide out for a disaster appeal impacts donor conversion for website visitors compared with a sticky bar appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a sticky donate button on the website with a slide-out disaster appeal CTA will increase donor conversion compared with a sticky bar appeal by reducing donor friction and increasing the urgency of making a gift during a natural disaster
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/01/2023 - 09/23/2023
CBN’s Operation Blessing provides critical disaster relief supplies to communities devastated by natural disasters. Typically, when a disaster strikes, they place an urgent donation appeal sticky bar message on their website to promote giving. They team wanted to see if this was the most effective tactic for increasing donor conversion during disasters.
We hypothesized that the sticky bar could easily be missed due to “banner blindness” on the site, especially with how busy their pages were with many CTAs, images, etc. We decided to test a sticky donate button that would be on the right side of the screen and slide out a disaster relief appeal message with “yes” “no” buttons. We believed this would reduce friction and increase the urgency of the disaster appeal, leading to higher donor conversion.
The control message read, “Give today to help provide disaster relief for those affected by Hurricane Idalia.”
The treatment message read, “Hi there, can you take 30-seconds to provide clean water, hot food, and critical supplies to those impacted by Hurricane Idalia? 100% of your gift goes to meeting their needs.” It would slide out automatically after the user was on the page for 7 seconds. We ran the experiment for 5 days with traffic split evenly between both experiences.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that implementing a sticky donate button with a slide-out disaster appeal message significantly increases donor conversion compared to a sticky bar appeal. The treatment message, which included a more personalized and urgent appeal, resulted in a 39.4% increase in donations with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence.

When digging into results by segment, every single segment showed significant donor preference for the sticky button vs control.

Here are results with level of confidence (LOC) by segment:
Mobile: 56% increased donor conversion at a 100% LOC (145 vs 93 donations)
 	Desktop: 33% increased donor conversion at a 99% LOC (162 vs 123 donations)
 	New visitors: 20% increased donor conversion at a 94% LOC (146 vs 122 donations)
 	Returning visitors: 60% increased donor conversion at a 100% LOC (172 vs 107 donations)

experiments.title: How detailing the benefits of donating any amount using a monthly giving premium and incentive language impacted recurring donor giving and overall page conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Our hypothesis is that clarifying the benefits associated with giving any amount, while also presenting the benefits of a monthly donation, will increase the number of donors choosing the monthly donation option on the Superbook giving page. This will increase the overall donation revenue without negatively impacting the conversion rate on the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2024 - 02/19/2024
CBN is a large Christian media organization that co-produces a popular animated series called Superbook. CBN Animation’s SuperBook series has been seen by millions of children worldwide and is one of CBN’s most effective ministries for children and families.
A few months ago we tested new language on the Superbook donation page that exists to acquire new donors who want to support upcoming seasons of Superbook. In that test we added new language to the page that detailed the benefits that a user would receive if they gave to CBN as a monthly donor instead of a one-time donor. The goal of that test was to increase recurring donor conversion on the page – and it was successful! We validated a 50% increase in recurring giving, but that lift came with a valid decrease in overall conversion on the page. The full test write-up can be found here: https://app.winstonknows.com/e/ewBd
In this test, we want to determine whether it’s possible to add language that clarifies the benefits of monthly giving and increase monthly donor conversion without negatively impacting overall conversion on the page. Our hypothesis is that, if we incentive people to make a recurring gift to support Superbook while also clarifying the benefits associated with giving a gift of any amount we can drive more monthly donor conversion without hurting one-time giving.
Both pages in this test featured the same value proposition copy explaining what a donor’s gift would do, but the Treatment included some additional changes:
Instead of saying “Your gift right now will…”, the Treatment said “Your gift of any amount will…”
The Treatment also added a paragraph that said “As a bonus gift, for a monthly donation of $20 or more, in addition to all Superbook episodes, you will receive streaming access to CBN’s Partner Exclusive library of teachings, devotionals and partner premiums to help your family grow in their faith.”
We set up both versions of the page in VWO and split the traffic 50/50. CBN used ads, emails, and site content to push traffic to the page and ran the experiment for roughly 2 weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this test is that clarifying the benefits associated with giving a gift of any amount, while also presenting the benefits of a monthly donation, significantly increased the number of donors who chose to make a monthly gift on the Superbook giving page. This change in language resulted in a 138.4% increase in Recurring Donation for All Traffic with a 99.9% statistical level of confidence.

In future experimentation, we should continue to emphasize the benefits of monthly giving while also highlighting the impact of giving any amount. In our next experiment we also want to test whether setting the default gift amount in the giving array to $20 will increase conversion and average gift, since right now the page is asking for a gift of $20 but the giving array defaults to $0.

experiments.title: How strengthening the value proposition of a homepage donation CTA impacts clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding increased clarity to the value proposition language around the homepage CTA will achieve an increase in clicks to the donation page by providing users with a better understanding of why they should make a gift and what their gift will actually do.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/17/2023
CBN is a Christian news, media, and distribution organization with a mission to preach the Gospel and demonstrate God’s love for the people of the world. The homepage of their website features an image with a Call-to-Action (CTA) that encourages readers to make a gift to CBN and support their work to share Christ’s love with the world.
For this test, we wanted to test into the impact of enhancing the value proposition language around this CTA by adding additional clarity, appeal, and credibility. We ran this test with the following variants:
Control: This variant featured a short and simple headline that said “Sharing the Love of Christ Around the World” above the “GIVE NOW” button
Treatment #1: This variant was designed to give readers a better understanding of how CBN shares Christ’s love by explaining that the reader’s gift to CBN will provide humanitarian aid and the Gospel to people. The headline in this variant said “Your gift right now will reach people with humanitarian aid and the Gospel of Jesus”
Treatment #2: Like Treatment #1, this variant added clarity to the headline, but it also added body copy that explained the problem more, starting with “Millions of people still lack access to the Gospel, hot food, and clean water but you can help change that.” It also reassured readers that 100% of their donation would support CBN’s mission. The final change made to this variant was that it would not display the hero image to mobile users. Instead, the headline and CTA would be displayed on a simple colored background to make it easier to read on mobile devices.
Our hypothesis is that the additional clarity provided in Treatment #1 and Treatment #2 would cause more users to click the “GIVE NOW” button and go to the donation page. We theorized that Treatment #2 would perform the best in this experiment because it added the most clarity to the value proposition language and was designed to make that language easier to read for mobile users.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that enhancing the value proposition language around the Call-to-Action (CTA) on the homepage had a significant impact on user engagement. The treatment variant, which added clarity, appeal, and credibility to the value proposition, resulted in a 111.3% increase in clicks to the donation page.

These findings suggest that users responded positively to the added information about how their gift will be used to provide humanitarian aid and share the Gospel. Additionally, the inclusion of body copy that highlighted the problem and reassured readers about the use of their donation likely increased trust and motivation to give.

In preparing for this experiment, one concern we had was that the length of variation two could create too much friction and that web visitors would instead prefer the shorter copy of version 1. The results however found the opposite. The longer copy provided even more details about the impact of a donor's gift, leading to higher donor conversion. While running this experiment, we began testing these value propositions on the donation page as well. The visitors in those experiments were excluded from this one to keep the tests clean, but we will use those tests to further refine the value proposition provided for donating to CBN.

We attempted to dig into donor conversion on the main donation page, but had an issue with the UTMs being stripped on the traffic coming to the page from this CTA.

experiments.title: How using interest and donor lookalike targeting improved response rates when compared to just targeting individuals who liked similar causes
experiments.researchQuestion: How do interest-based targeted audiences and donor lookalike modeled audiences perform relative to targeting fans of similar causes with your Facebook campaigns?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/24/2019
As a part of the acquisition efforts on Facebook to promote Global Cat Day for Alley Cat Allies, we made an interesting observation when compiling the results of how various audiences performed through the campaign effort.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Overview:
With 100% level of confidence, we observed that donor lookalike modeled audiences were +334.9% more likely to become a donor than simply just targeting "the competition's audience" by including "similar causes" in our targeting.
Further, we observed that the interest based audiences also showed a lift over targeting fans of a similar cause with a +26% lift in donor conversion (although that finding was not validated, but directional).
The numbers on paper don't look that wide when comparing results from the interest targeting (in treatment #2) vs. donor lookalike results (in treatment #3), however the difference is that the donor lookalike modeled audience actually produced a +243.6% increase in donor conversion rate (LoC: 100%) just between those two segments, as well.
Conclusion:
At first glance, it might appear that the only audience that you should target would be the donor lookalike modeled audiences for your acquisition efforts based upon these results. However, we would argue that the decision about which audiences you should target is not a question of "IF" but rather "WHEN."
If you are a smaller organization, with a very small budget, that is looking to mitigate as much risk as you can, while maximizing your opportunity to generate the highest likelihood of return on investment, then you should likely only target donor lookalike modeled audiences.
But, if you are looking to expand your reach into new audiences and give yourself a chance at a higher volume of opportunity, then we would recommend layering in interest-based targets (and even similar cause targeting).
But it appears that when launching a campaign from scratch, that targeting the fans of similar causes alone is potentially far less effective than by targeting people that Facebook believes look like your donors (but are not today).

experiments.title: How explaining the root causes of an issue affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does explaining the “root cause” increase donor conversion rates (or revenue) as a part of the Global Cat Day campaign?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/10/2019 - 10/21/2019
Alley Cat Allies, as a part of their annual Global Cat Day campaign, asked for supporters and prospects to pledge to be advocates for all cats everywhere, but especially those that do not have people to defend them today. This initial step helped align the audience around a common goal while also collecting contact information for prospective donors.
After supporters signed the pledge, they were taken to an instant donation page that asked them to take the next step towards saving cats lives.
In writing the donation page, we wondered if explaining the root causes for why things need to change was important for us to drive donor conversion rate, or revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Findings:
With a 95.8% level of confidence, we observed a +14.2% increase in donor conversion rate with the version of the donation page that did not explain the root causes.
Although we did not validate this portion of the experiment, we also observed that the revenue also increased by +17.5% (with a level of confidence of 93.4%).
Key Learnings:
This experiment implies that over-explaining root causes can distract donors from what they really want to do with their gift... which is to solve the problem (or create the impact).
When splitting the experiment data between NEW VISITORS and RETURNING VISITORS, we made a few observations, as well.
For NEW VISITORS, we observed an even greater lift overall (+18.1% in donor conversion rate with a 95.6% level of confidence). This is where most of the data that was used to validate the overall experiment was produced. This is interesting, because NEW VISITORS are most likely to be unaware of what the root causes to this problem were when compared to returning visitors (who have already visited the website), logic might lead you to believe that they needed that information to make a gift.
For them to be more responsive to the shorter version (that did not explain the root causes), is very telling; it proves that they (the uninitiated to the problem's root causes) didn't need that information to make a gift.
For RETURNING VISITORS, we observed a directional increase with the shorter copy of just +4.6% in donor conversion rate, but the experiment did not validate, as it produced only a 33% level of confidence.
What this means is that the copy used to describe the root cause issues was also (at least directionally) less effective than focusing these returning visitors on the outcomes (instead of spending time to tell them what caused the problem in the first place). However, since it did not validate, further experimentation may be required moving forward to verify this learning with returning visitors.
Conclusion:
Overall, the key learning from this experiment appears to be that we do not need to spend too much time (or copy) on the donation page during a campaign like this explaining why the problem exists, but rather focus the audience on what they can do to solve the problem(s) we're talking about.

experiments.title: How adding an impact callout to a pre-selected monthly tab affects recurring donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a callout on the monthly tab, highlighting the impact of monthly giving for donation page visitors, will increase recurring donations because the callout will help users understand the potential impact they can have on the FUS mission and encourage them to make a recurring donation.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/26/2025 - 10/08/2025
Franciscan University has content offers of their video series’ running on Facebook, which perform really well. They get a lot of donations from these offers, so we wanted to look into ways we could get more recurring donors from audiences coming to the donation pages.
For this test, we had the monthly tab pre-selected and we added a callout to hover above the tab that had language about how monthly giving was the most impactful way they could help FUS continue doing their mission.
This was tested on the donation page of the offers currently running for them in Facebook.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:
1. Adding a callout on the monthly tab highlighting the impact of monthly giving led to a 1.5% increase in Recurring Donations for All Traffic at a 100.0% level of statistical confidence.
2. However, this increase in recurring donations was accompanied by a 211% decrease in Transactions with a 96% level of confidence and a 411% decrease in One-Time Donations with a 100% level of confidence.
In future experimentation, it is crucial to carefully weigh the benefits of increasing recurring donations against the significant decline observed in one-time donations and transactions. Further analysis of why one-time donations and transactions dropped could reveal ways to promote recurring giving without causing large declines in other donation types.
Test different callout messages or positions to optimize recurring donations without hurting other metrics. Segment the audience by behavior and preferences to tailor the approach and maximize total donations.
Continue experimenting with new strategies and closely monitor all donation metrics. Aim to increase recurring donations while maintaining overall donation levels.

experiments.title: How a personalized sender impacts open and click-through rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a personalized sender for Franciscan University’s emails will achieve an increase in opens and click-thru rate because it humanizes the entire interaction—addressing the reader by name and writing from the perspective of a real person. because it humanizes the entire interaction—addressing the reader by name and writing from the perspective of a real person.

They do not address the reader by name with a salutation, are written in a third-person tone, and do not come from a specific sender at the University. We hypothesized that personalizing this experience for the reader: addressing them by name in the opening, sending from a real person, and humanizing the email copy so it read like an email rather than a newsletter would increase opens and click-thru rate.

The key learning from this experiment is that personalizing the sender for Franciscan University's emails had a significant impact on the open rates, resulting in a 33.5% increase in opens for all traffic with a 100.0% level of statistical confidence. This outcome suggests that humanizing the email interaction by addressing the reader by name and sending from a real person resonated positively with the recipients and encouraged them to engage more with the emails.

Additionally, our second hypothesis is that this would increase click-thru rate as well as opens. While just below statistical validity, Franciscan also saw a 12% increase in click-thru rate with a 93.9% level of confidence.

In future experimentation, it is advisable to continue personalizing the sender for Franciscan University's emails to maintain or even further improve the open and click-thru rates. However, it may be worthwhile to determine whether this approach results in the same effect on Faith and Reason emails—which users are more accustomed to receiving from the organization itself. Regularly testing and analyzing different tactics will provide valuable insights for optimizing email communications and achieving the desired outcomes.

experiments.title: What we can learn from testing multiple elements within an email
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a simplified email design increase email engagement?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/21/2018
The Museum of the Bible had a significantly large email list acquired from visitor to the museum. However, due to system issues, this email file had not been sent any significant communications for multiple months. Leading up to the year end fundraising campaign, they wanted to send some initial communications to start warming the file back up. However, past emails from the Museum were heavily designed. We decided to run an experiment to see if simplifying the email design would improve overall email engagement.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Unfortunately, system limitations resulted in an experiment that we are not able to pull conclusive findings from. This is the case for several reasons:
Both emails were "designed." While the control had more design elements, the treatment still made it clear that it was a marketing email. The goal of a "simplified email" experiment is to strip any elements from the email that would indicate it was sent from a marketing email system.
The personalization used in the treatment email was good (i.e. "Dear Chelcie") but, since it was used below an HTML banner, it would not have been visible in the preview text of the email. Before a subscriber opened the email, they would have seen the alternative text for the banner rather than their name. This would have been another indicator that it was a marketing email.
Using a raw URL in an email is a good way to make it feel more genuine but, when that URL isn't linked, it is not possible for the email to track the clicks. While the treatment shows a 92% decrease in clicks, we have no real way of knowing how many people copied the URL and visited the video. Put simply, we don't know the true impact of the raw URL versus the image of the video.

experiments.title: Can a Streamlined Donation Path Improve Results for a Content Offer?
experiments.researchQuestion: By removing the initial email capture step and directing high-intent visitors straight to the donation page via a slide-up prompt, we believe we can reduce conversion friction, shorten the donor journey, and increase overall donation revenue without significantly sacrificing lead generation for the digital magazine.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/10/2025 - 06/25/2025
This experiment was conducted for Catholic Extension Society (CES), a nonprofit organization that supports Catholic faith communities in under-resourced areas. The campaign centered around a special content offer tied to Extension, CES’s quarterly magazine. This particular edition was a commemorative issue celebrating the newly appointed pope.
The primary offer allowed supporters to sign up to receive the digital edition when it becomes available. Additionally, anyone who donated $35 or more would receive a physical copy of the magazine as a thank-you gift.
The experiment aimed to test whether adding a slide-up that bypassed the digital sign-up step—and instead directed visitors straight to the donation page—would reduce friction and result in more donations.
This test was split 50/50, and everything else remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment compared two user pathways to drive donations in exchange for a commemorative edition of Extension magazine:
Control: A landing page that promoted sign-up for the digital magazine, with an optional donation ask (gift array started below $35).
 	Variant (Direct Ask): A slide-up that bypassed the digital sign-up and directed users straight to a donation page (gift array starting at $35).
Key Takeaways
The Control significantly outperformed the Direct Ask in donor conversion rate—5.4% vs. 1.3%, representing a 75.4% drop in performance for the variant.
 	While the Direct Ask page aimed to remove friction by sending visitors straight to a donation form, it appears to have disrupted the user motivation flow. The digital magazine offer likely served as a stronger value proposition and entry point, especially for cold or less-committed visitors.
 	Despite a slightly higher average gift on the Direct Ask page, the sharp drop in conversion volume led to dramatically lower total revenue and engagement.
Insights & Interpretation
Donation Volume: The control page drove nearly 4x more qualified gifts (177 vs. 45) than the direct ask page. This suggests that leading with the magazine offer and allowing users to choose whether to donate afterward may generate more overall engagement and gift volume.
 	Average Gift Size: While the direct ask page had a higher average gift size ($48 vs. $42.71), the lift was not enough to offset the substantial decrease in total conversions.
 	Total Revenue: The control page significantly outperformed in total revenue from $35+ gifts, generating $7,560 vs. $1,680—a 350%+ increase over the direct ask version.
 	Friction Tradeoff: Although the direct ask aimed to reduce friction by shortening the donor journey, it may have missed the motivational hook provided by the digital content offer, resulting in lower engagement and fewer conversions overall.
Conclusion
substantially more revenue and donor conversions
leading with a high-value content offer

experiments.title: How reinforcing security on a campaign donation page donor conversion and overall revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a statement of security to the year end donation page will increase donor conversion and overall revenue, as it enhances clarity regarding how a potential donor’s data is being used.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/23/2025 - 04/30/2025
America Needs Fatima ran a calendar year-end campaign during December. With increasing concerns about privacy and user data, the team wanted to test whether adding a statement about the organization’s privacy commitment on the donation page would affect transactions and overall campaign revenue. The next step is to analyze the test results to determine whether the privacy statement affected donor behavior or revenue. The test revealed that including a privacy statement had a measurable effect on donation page conversions and total year-end campaign revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a statement of security on the donation page significantly increased donor conversion and overall revenue. The treatment group, which included the statement of security, saw a 50.5% increase in revenue compared to the control group. This result was statistically significant at a 98.5% confidence level.
This suggests that potential donors highly value transparency and clarity regarding the use of their data. By addressing their concerns about privacy and security, we were able to gain their trust and increase their willingness to make a donation.
In future experiments, consider adding a statement regarding security or privacy on donation pages. Use this on any page where user data is collected. This can help alleviate concerns and increase conversion rates. Further explore other ways to enhance transparency and build trust with donors. Regularly test and iterate on these strategies to boost revenue.

experiments.title: How removing an image in a fundraising direct appeal email impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the image for a direct appeal email will achieve an increase in clicks to the donation page because it will simplify the email, reduce distractions, and focus the reader’s attention on the call to action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/25/2024 - 11/25/2024
America Needs Fatima’s Fatima Statue Custodians travel across the country, bringing the statue of Our Lady of Fatima into homes to foster prayer, healing, and spiritual renewal. To sustain this mission, they launched a fundraising campaign to cover rising fuel costs and tested how an email image might impact engagement.
In a previous test, we found that removing an image of a Custodian visit significantly decreased clicks, suggesting that the image played a key role in engagement. However, we wanted to further explore whether this finding applied across different types of images.
For this follow-up test, we examined whether an image of the sender, Mr. Ferrera, an active Fatima Statue Custodian, standing with the statue of Our Lady, would help or hinder email engagement. The Control email included this image, while the Treatment removed it.
We hypothesized that removing the image would increase clicks by simplifying the email, reducing distractions, and keeping the reader focused on the call to action.
This was a 50/50 A/B test, with half of the audience receiving the Control email (with the image) and the other half receiving the Treatment email (without the image).
experiments.keyLearnings:
Surprisingly, removing the image of the sender led to a 59.6% increase in email clicks, with a 97% statistical level of confidence. This result contradicts the first test, where removing an image of a Custodian visit led to a significant decrease in clicks.

This suggests that not all images contribute equally to engagement—context and content matter. Several psychological principles may help explain this outcome:
Personal Connection vs. Mission Focus: While personal images can build trust, they can also shift focus away from the larger mission. The first test’s image reinforced the mission (showing a Custodian in action), whereas this test’s image may have felt less necessary.
 	Reader Expectations: Donors might be more compelled by an image that directly reinforces the impact of their gift (e.g., a Custodian at work) rather than one featuring the sender.
testing different image types

experiments.title: How a less designed, text heavy email affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a less designed email lead to more giving?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/01/2020
KUOW Public Radio is Seattle’s NPW news station. Their independent, nonprofit newsroom produces award-winning stories, podcasts and events. For a time sensitive fundraising campaign, KUOW decided to test their usual email design against a more simple, stripped down, less designed version to see if it would lead to more donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The stripped down, plain text email led to a 28.8% increase in response rate which was the main goal/focus of the experiment and suggests that KUOW should consider this approach again in future email appeals.
A few other interesting findings:
There was a -24% difference in average gift (mostly driven by two larger than normal donations) but is something worth monitoring in future experiments to ensure this approach leads not only to more donations but more overall revenue.The designed email was 28% better at generating clicks or getting people to the donation page (100% confidence) but produced fewer donations overall which means the click to conversion rate of the stripped down email was actually 79.4% greater at converting clicks to donations (100% confidence). This goes to show that often what gets clicks isn't what gets donations or, as is the case here, actually gets fewer donations.
It seems clear that a stripped down, plain text approach can work for KUOW so now they can begin testing other elements within this strategy like the tone, raw links, referencing past giving, the use of images, length of copy, etc.

experiments.title: How does longer form email copy affects clickthrough rate and donation conversion rate?
experiments.researchQuestion: Which email will generated the highest clickthrough rate and ultimately the most donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/16/2010 - 12/16/2010
Like many nonprofit organizations, the Colson Center had adapted their email strategy from their direct mail strategy.  They sent very long letters from the organization’s founder in mail, and so when they launched their email appeal program, they took the very same approach.  But are email and direct mail one in the same?   In this experiment, the Colson Center challenged this paradigm and generated a new treatment that used dramatically less copy than the control.  Once the email recipient clicked to the landing page, they would receive essentially the same copy as the longform email version, but would be one step closer to giving.  The hypothesis is this: by lowering the perceived friction (length of copy) in the email, can we increase visits to the donation landing page and thereby increase giving?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of this experiment are very interesting because while clickthrough increased dramatically by over 1,200%, the increase in traffic did not significantly affect donations.  Through other experiments we have learned that when it comes to email optimization, we have two dials that we can move that are codependent: Traffic and Conversion Rate.  For example, we can increase Traffic by removing friction from the email, but that also means that the traffic we send is going to be much less motivated to take action and thereby producing a much lower Conversion Rate on the landing page.  If the goal is to increase the total net donations or donation revenue, that sacrifice in Conversion Rate may be okay as long as the increase in Traffic Volume yield additional net donations and revenue.  In this particular case, that was not the results so we concluded that we needed to test this same concept again during the next appeal month.

experiments.title: How Meta Campaign Optimization Goals Affect Donor Acquisition Costs
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that optimizing Meta ad campaigns for donations rather than email sign-ups for prospective supporters reached through Meta Ad campaigns will achieve more cost-effective donor acquisition because signals of high intent help ad platforms prioritize users who are more psychologically ready to give, not just willing to exchange an email address.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/19/2025 - 01/04/2026
Global Christian Relief (GCR) has a strategic priority to grow its email subscriber base as a pathway to long-term donor growth. While acquiring email addresses at a low cost is important, the team recognizes a core tension: not all subscribers are equally likely to become donors. This experiment is designed to explore how acquisition strategy influences downstream donor quality, not just volume.
Specifically, this test examined whether Meta’s ad optimization goal affects the cost-effectiveness of acquiring future donors through a content offer funnel. The central question is whether optimizing campaigns for email sign-ups (names) or for donations (purchases) ultimately leads to lower donor acquisition costs.
The goal of this experiment is to determine whether optimizing earlier in the funnel for volume (email sign-ups) or later in the funnel for intent (donations) results in more cost-effective donor acquisition over time. The findings will inform how GCR balances scale and quality in its paid acquisition strategy and how it approaches list growth as a driver of sustainable fundraising.
To test this, GCR promoted a single content offer through Meta ad properties, including Facebook, Instagram, and Threads. Ads were duplicated across two campaigns that were identical in creative, audience, and spend, with the only variable being the Meta Pixel optimization goal. One campaign was optimized for email sign-ups (email optimization, serving as the control), while the other was optimized for donations (purchase optimization, serving as the treatment).
The audience for both campaigns was built using a combination of existing GCR donors and a lookalike audience derived from that donor data, ensuring the ads reach people with a higher likelihood of alignment and potential generosity. Ad spend was split evenly between the two campaigns to allow for a fair comparison.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Optimizing Meta campaigns for donations rather than email sign-ups led to significantly stronger donor outcomes. While the email-optimized campaign generated more overall traffic, the purchase-optimized campaign produced more than four times the donation conversion rate (0.47% vs. 0.03%) and drove 9 of the 11 total donations, despite receiving fewer impressions and a smaller sample size.
The relative difference between the two approaches was substantial: the purchase-optimized campaign outperformed the email-optimized campaign by 1,480%, with a 99.4% level of statistical confidence, exceeding the threshold required for a valid result. This indicates that the observed performance difference is highly unlikely to be due to chance.
We also measured the Gross Cost Per Donor (GCPD) as a key metric in this test. The email optimized campaign yielded a GCPD increase of 347%—further proof that optimizing for donations in Meta Ads campaigns is a much more efficient use of ad spend than optimizing for more top-of-funnel conversions.
These results suggest that Meta’s optimization signals meaningfully influence who is delivered into the funnel, not just how many people enter it. When the platform is instructed to optimize for donations, it appears to prioritize users who are psychologically closer to making a financial commitment, even when the initial interaction is a content offer rather than a direct donation ask. In contrast, optimizing for email sign-ups likely attracts lower-intent users who are willing to exchange an email address but are less prepared to give.
Importantly, this test highlights a key tradeoff in list growth strategy. While optimizing for email acquisition may reduce cost per subscriber and increase list volume, it can significantly dilute downstream donor quality. For Global Christian Relief, where the ultimate goal is list growth, optimizing for higher-intent actions earlier in the funnel is more effective.
The implication for future campaigns is clear: when donor acquisition is the primary objective, optimizing paid media for donations—even within an indirect, content-driven funnel—can yield fewer but substantially more valuable supporters. This finding supports a shift away from volume-first acquisition metrics and toward optimization strategies that reflect true donor intent and long-term fundraising value.
Open questions remain around scale and efficiency: how this approach performs at higher spend levels, whether similar patterns hold across different offers or audiences, and how email engagement and lifetime value differ between cohorts acquired under each optimization goal. These areas represent strong candidates for follow-up testing.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an abandoned cart pop-up for users who had begun making a gift on the main donation page but abandoned the process will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it will help recapture traffic that would have otherwise left their gift incomplete.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/07/2024 - 05/20/2024
To increase conversion on NPH’s main donation page, we hypothesized that an abandoned cart pop-up would help recapture donors who had arrived on the main donate, started making a gift, but abandoned the process for one reason or another. If the user navigated to a different portion of the site, or simply X’d out and returned at a later time, they would receive a prompt that asked them to finish making their donation. The pop-up was set to dynamically pull the gift amount the user had previously selected as well as the frequency, allowing them to resume where they had left off.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that implementing an abandoned cart pop-up for users who had started making a gift on the main donation page but abandoned the process resulted in an increase in Recurring Donation for All Traffic. This increase was statistically significant at a 97.5% confidence level, indicating that the treatment was effective in recapturing potential donors and encouraging them to complete their donation.

Although the control and treatment had a similar donor conversion rate when considering transactions overall, this experiment reveals that it was most impactful at recapturing recurring donations that would've otherwise been lost. There are many things to consider when making a monthly commitment. A user may have to reflect on what their budget allows them to commit to each month or the best day of the month for it to come out. It's likely that any of these could be why users were originally abandoning the monthly donation before committing. Have that reinforced with a pop-up and made simple by carrying over the original amount and frequency was the prompt they needed to complete the transaction.

experiments.title: How emphasizing the “give button” in the main navigation affects donor conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding emphasis to a give button in the main navigation for all visitors to the site will achieve a greater number of donor conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/21/2022 - 07/16/2022
ABWE (Association of Baptists for World Evangelism) is a global family of ministries that exists to fulfill the Great Commission by multiplying leaders, churches, and missions movements among every people. ABWE wanted to test if adding more emphasis to a “give button” on the navigation would lead to a greater number of donor conversions. For this experiment, we tested adding contrast to the give button on the main navigation (the treatment). For the control we left the give button in it’s natural state.
experiments.keyLearnings:
As hypothesized, the treatment saw an increase in donor conversions of nearly 24%. The experiment received both enough sample size (32k) and a high enough level of confidence (99%) to validate. In order for experiments to be statically significant, both of these factors must be present. In this case, both elements were true, and the treatment won. Next steps in this experiment will be to run the treatment as a control, and test the placement of the emphasized give button.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Desktop visitors had a 19.9% increase in donations with a 93% level of confidence.
 	Mobile visitors had a 31.6% increase in donations with a 96% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How form field friction affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will reducing the number of form fields increase email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/14/2016 - 08/10/2016
Food for the Poor was using Google ads to promote their “Verse of the Day” email. When the visitor signed up for the email, they asked for four pieces of information: First Name, Last Name, Email, and ZIP code. Though this was typical of email acquisition pages, they wanted to see if only asking for email and ZIP code would increase email acquisition rate. They developed a treatment that reduced the form fields to two and launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment produced a 48.3% increase in email conversion rate. This shows us that asking for less information can result in more signups. However, there is a trade-off to consider in that Food for the Poor will not be able to personalize their emails unless first and last name are eventually collected. For a simple email like “Verse of the Day”, such personalization may not be necessary.

experiments.title: How an individual sender impacts the open rate of an organizational email
experiments.researchQuestion: How will an individual sender impact email engagement?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/28/2017 - 12/04/2017
For Giving Tuesday, the Competitive Enterprise Institute was sending out a fundraising email to their house email file. Historically, these fundraising emails had come from the organization instead of an individual. We wanted to see if sending from the organization’s president, Kent Lassman, would be able to improve the email results. Since it was coming from an individual, we also modified the email so that it had a more personal tone.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment resulted in a 27.5% increase in opens for the house email file. We believe this is the result of two factors from the email: the sender and the first sentence. Prior research has found that sending from an individual increases the likelihood to open the email. Additionally, by modifying the first sentence, we changed what was viewed in the preview pane of the email. In this case, we were able to incorporate the individual’s name so that the email appeared to be send from an individual to an individual instead of from an organization to a wide list of people.

experiments.title: How urgency affects clickthrough rate in website popups
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing clarity of the call-to-action increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/03/2016 - 05/16/2016
Run with Water was raising entry fees before their race event. They used a ‘welcome mat’ pop-up to let visitors know and encourage them to register for the race. However, they hypothesized that time-sensitive language that highlighted the user’s benefit might increase urgency and increase race registrations instead of the cause-based call-to-action. So they created a treatment that highlighted the deadline and presented the benefit to the user, rather than cause-based copy.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment with urgency-based language delivered a 24.8% increase in clickthrough rate. This shows that while cause-based copy might inspire some people (like existing donors or constituents familiar with the cause), clarity trumps persuasion. While we must continually test, research shows that plainly stating the benefits can often increase conversion in place of inspirational copy.

experiments.title: How a softer subject line impacts open rate

rows.name: Make Your Reservations for Our Upcomi...
Announcing a New Event at the Reagan...

experiments.researchQuestion: Which subject line will get the most subscribers to engage?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/30/2017 - 06/13/2017
The Reagan Presidential Library was promoting their summer events through a promotional email series. They had historically used the same subject line “Make Your Reservations for Our Upcoming Events at the Reagan Library.”
They decided to test out a new approach to this subject line by using one that did not use an action the reader had to take in the test: “Announcing a New Event at the Reagan Library!”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment resulted in a 8.9% increase in overall email opens. This increased lift in opens led to an 8.3% increase in the number of visitors (clicks) to the website.
Since the control asks the reader to “Make their reservation,” those not willing to make that commitment were less inclined to open it when they received it.
By eliminating the immediate request for action and focusing the subject line solely on what someone get’s (or get’s to discover, in this case), utilizing mystery, we convinced approximately 9% more readers to open.
Had there not been any mystery in the original subject line, it is possible that the difference in performance would be substantially greater.

experiments.title: How reducing friction on mobile devices affects name conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does reducing copy on mobile devices increase the likelihood of conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/12/2014 - 07/23/2015
Human Liberty was promoting a petition directed at the International Justice system in support of North Koreans held in captivity by a totalitarian government. They noticed that much of their high-converting traffic was coming from mobile devices. Conversion was relatively low, even for the highly-targeted audiences they focused their advertising on. So, they proposed a test to discover exactly how highly motivated the visitors were. They created a new treatment with drastically shorter copy. If the audience was already highly motivated, the additional copy on the control might actually be slowing them down on a mobile device.
experiments.keyLearnings: The new treatment increased conversion by 76.2% — validating the hypothesis that mobile traffic was not only highly motivated and needed less “convincing”, but also responded well to shorter messages that took less time to read. This was highly valuable for all of Human Liberty’s mobile-focused campaigns.

experiments.title: How clarifying the process-level value proposition affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: What role does self esteem play in conversion?  By modifying the copy to be more inclusive, can we get more people to accept the offer?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/20/2014 - 04/30/2014
As part of an email acquisition campaign, Good of All offered a free eBook to visitors in exchange for their name and email.  With the majority of the traffic to the landing page coming directly from Facebook ads, visitors to the landing page were highly motivated and already converting at a high rate.  The goal of this test was to learn how changes to the copy and call to action affect the email acquisition conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: By addressing the reader as a fellow “world-changer” and focusing on what the visitor “gets” rather than what they must “do”, the conversion rate improved by 133.7%. We need to remember that visitors to our landing pages are human beings with feelings and emotions.  The copy we put forth on our landing pages should be designed to embrace those feelings and our offers must be framed around the result (what the visitor gets), not the action required (what they must do).

First United Church is a church committed at its heart to the struggle for social justice and is a place of welcome for people experiencing homelessness and poverty in Vancouver’s Downtown Eastside. This email was focused on securing donated items and asking supporters to download the latest list of needs.
experiments.keyLearnings: When a clear call to action exists within the email, adding an action statement, like ‘join us’, can help the reader qualify if they want to open and read knowing there is an action and if they do they are much more likely to click and take that action.

experiments.title: How removing the recommended donation amount increased the perceived value of an offer
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing the recommended gift amount affect donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/20/2017 - 04/07/2017
Every Home for Christ was offering their book Look What God is Doing as a free offer for donors and potential donors to develop a deeper understanding of the impact that their organization was having across the globe.
After someone requested the book, they were shown a Thank You page with a with a follow-up donation ask. In the control version of this page, Every Home for Christ was recommending a $10 donation. In exchange for this donation, they would send the donor a free copy of another book titled The Hour That Changes the World.
They had the hypothesis that this recommended giving level could be keeping donors from giving at their full capacity. So they created two treatments. The first treatment recommended a $25 donation. The second treatment did not recommend an amount, but left an open field for the donor to give a gift of any amount.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Treatment 2, the version without a donation recommendation, saw a 276% increase in donations. This tells us that the initial recommended gift amount may have been holding their donors and potential donors back from giving at their full potential.
By removing the recommendation, the value of the offer was increased in the mind of the donor, leading to a higher conversion rate.
From here, Every Home for Christ will be monitoring the rate at which these donors give a 2nd gift in order to determine if this winning treatment has any effect on the longevity of the donor.

experiments.title: How removing the email template impacts clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a plain text email generate more clicks?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/03/2018
During Hurricane Florence, BGEA experimented with sending a plain text style email against their standard marketing email template to see if it generated more clicks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In reviewing the two creatives that were involved in this experiment, we initially thought that the marketing email generated more clicks, but after further slicing the web analytics details, we identified that the marketing template had an additional clickable link to an article (which was hyperlinked on the header image) that the plain text version did not include.
Overall clicks were higher on the marketing template, but when you isolate the click traffic on the PRIMARY objective (which is calling the recipient to give a gift), we actually saw that the treatment generated 1,025 clicks against the control’s 526 clicks on the “Give Now” option (which represents a 94% increase in click activity).
Subsequently, the clickthrough traffic on that primary call to action (Give Now) saw a higher conversion rate (16.6%), as well as a 53.1% higher overall revenue than the control.
Further experimentation is required to validate this hypothesis, but initial results look promising.

experiments.title: How visitor motivation affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which landing page treatment will lead to the most donations and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/11/2015 - 08/14/2015
The Media Research Center (MRC) was running a petition to the major news networks to increase their coverage of the Planned Parenthood scandal. After the initial signature, the visitors were sent to a thank you page and given the opportunity to make a gift.  The page was a two-column design in keeping with layout of the previous petition page.  The left side had value proposition language relating the media coverage to the Gosnell trail and the initial Planned Parenthood video embedded in the copy.
It was our hypothesis that we were assuming too much with the visitors. By placing the donation form at the top of the page, we were visually making an ask without first giving the visitors a reason to give. Additionally, the copy focused on what MRC had done in the past instead of what it was currently doing to combat the Planned Parenthood media outage.  As a result, we created a new treatment that utilized a single column design, removed the video from the page, and tweaked the copy to focus on Planned Parenthood instead of Goznell.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The single column landing page resulted in a 32% decrease in donor conversion. It is our hypothesis this decrease is less about the page layout and value proposition used and more about the motivation of the visitors. With a campaign topic as emotional as the Planned Parenthood scandal, the visitors were already highly motivated to give. By moving the donation form down on the page, we added additional friction to the process. The visitors did not need the additional reasons to give so when they didn’t see the donation form, the abandoned the process.
Most landing pages will see increased results when we place the value proposition copy before the donation form however, it is important to recognize the influence that external factors have on the donors’ motivation to give.

experiments.title: How the landing page layout affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which layout will produce the greatest number of donations for the year end campaign?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/17/2012 - 01/01/2013
As part of their year end fundraising campaign, Media Research Center wanted to find the best way to communicate their unique value proposition that would lead to increased giving. The standard donation page layout had always been a two column approach in order to get the donation form closer to the top of the page. We wanted to see if that was the best approach to communicate the case for support to the potential donors.
experiments.keyLearnings: By moving the donation form below the form, we allowed the value proposition communicated within the copy to better “sell” the gift. The single column layout was effectively making an ask before adequately explaining why the gift was needed.  The test did not have a high enough level of confidence to be proven valid so further testing would need to be done to fully validate these results.

experiments.title: How the popularity of 3rd party credibility can affect conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which visual layout or 3rd party sources will produce the greatest conversion rate for the donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/12/2015 - 07/31/2015
For their primary donation page, the Media Research Center wanted to find what 3rd party sources lend the most credibility to their value proposition. Many conservative figures cite their research in their work but the different individuals have varying levels of popularity.
We created a test of the existing donation page and replaced a one public figure (Laura Ingraham) with another (Rush Limbaugh). The ordering was also changed up to bring the animated element of the page closer to the top. We wanted to see if this new arrangement would improve the conversion of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: By placing a more well-known figure, and his endorsement, closer to the donation form, we were able to reduce the anxiety of the donor enough to improve the conversion rate by 25%.

experiments.title: How the tone of the email can affect the donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which messaging tone in the email will produce the greatest number of donors and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/19/2015 - 08/24/2015
When the Planned Parenthood videos were first starting to be released, the Media Research Center launched a petition with the goal of getting the major media networks to give the story the level of attention it deserved. By the time the 7th video was released, this petition had acquired more than 53,000 signatures. With each subsequent video, we wanted to update these signers on the progress of the petition and give them a way to contribute further with a “Quick Donate” option.
Previous emails had all used a “negative” tone by focusing on the continued lack of media coverage and what was needed to overcome the problem. We had a hypothesis that if we led with a positive tone by acknowledging the signers’ past action and the resulting progress, we could inspire them to take the next step and give a gift.  We decided to split the email file and validate this hypothesis.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The more negative email was able to produce 11% more opens but both emails had the same number of people click to the landing page to give a gift. However, those clicking from the positive email were not as incentivized to give a gift which led to a 46% decrease in donor conversion. Those visitors that had been inspired by the work left to do (the “negative” tone) and the urgency of the appeal were more highly motivated to give a gift once they made it to the page.
It is critical to remember, in an email appeal, the tone of the entire campaign can be impacted by every step of the process.

experiments.title: How the value communicated in a link can affect donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Does communicating the value of the gift in the link increase the subsequent donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/25/2015 - 08/31/2015
Media Research Center was running a petition campaign to force the major news networks to spend more time covering the Planned Parenthood undercover videos. We had learned in previous tests that the signers of the petition were highly motivated individuals that did not need a lot of value proposition copy to be convinced to make a gift. As a result, we utilized a “quick donate” strategy in subsequent emails that let the donors select their donation amount in the email and skip directly to the donation page.
While this strategy was proving successful, we wanted to find a way to boost the average gift of the donors since the average gift was below what is normally seen. We had a hypothesis that adding value to the individuals links would inspire larger gifts. As a result, we created a new treatment that applied value proposition copy to the links as a method of communicating greater value to the donor.
experiments.keyLearnings: While this test did not reach the 95% level of confidence necessary to be considered statistically significant, it was quickly approaching a valid result. While additional testing will be necessary to be certain, the increase in donations and revenue would indicate that the value proposition language boosted the overall campaign results.

experiments.title: How the headline on a popup affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a headline that reinforces the value proposition of the offer convert more people?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/08/2018
Heartlight Ministries was offering a free two-week online course through an exit intent popup on their website. The control popup focused the headline on getting the free course, instead of focusing on the value proposition of the course. The remainder of the copy and design stayed the same, but the headline was tested.
experiments.keyLearnings: Although the test was not statistically valid, the value-focused headline treatment did produce a directional 9% lift in conversions. More testing is necessary, but this test suggests that focusing on the free-ness of the offer without explaining the value behind it produces a decrease in conversion, and that clarifying the value is key to them caring about the free course.

experiments.title: How adding a testimonial affects email sign ups on a landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will utilizing a review on a landing page increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/29/2020
Back to the Bible launched a new eBook 7 Secrets of Worry-Free Living and wanted to test whether including a review from the leader of a Christian men’s conference, Brian Doyle, would increase email sign ups on the landing page. All other landing page elements were identical.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we turned off the experiment before reaching a valid level of confidence, we were seeing an overall decline in conversion rate on the treatment. Future experiments will test using other various forms of social proof such as number of eBook downloads or a testimonial from a peer.

experiments.title: How strengthening the value proposition impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would strengthening the value proposition on the donation page impact donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/16/2019
Back to the Bible sent out an email to introduce the new Bible teacher for their radio broadcast. The email linked to a donation page that continued with the biographical information of the new Bible teacher and had a donation form at the bottom of the page. We hypothesized that if we were linking people to a donation page a stronger connection between the Bible teacher and the donation ask needed to be made in an effort to motivate people to make a gift.
experiments.keyLearnings: Engagement with the email appeal was fairly healthy, but when it came to giving from this, the audience was pretty lethargic. With only a couple of donations coming in from this email appeal, the donation page treatment had no impact on conversion because overall motivation was so low. What we learned from this experiment is that it is a far stretch to go from introducing an audience to someone they never knew in a few short paragraphs to then asking them for a donation. This kind of email would have better been suited as a cultivation piece rather than an appeal.

experiments.title: How shortening the value proposition copy on a high urgency donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that condensing the value proposition for potential donors on a monthly appeal page will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/21/2022 - 10/01/2022
The Boy Scouts of America ran a monthly appeal campaign in September that consisted of one email and one follow-up to that email. The first email only received two donations therefore we needed to test changing the language on the donation page to see if this would increase donor conversion rate.
We felt like the donation page copy on the original page was too long to read therefore causing friction when someone landed on this page to potentially convert.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment page saw a 43.7% decrease in donor conversion rate with a near-valid level of significance of 84.9%.
After sending the follow-up appeal that had more urgency within the copy and was shortened compared to the first email, we believed that the issue actually laid within the email content of the first email, not the original donation page. Therefore, by removing a lot of the value proposition from the treatment donation page, users coming from the second email did not have enough value given to them at this step in the funnel in order to convince them to make a donation.
Next, we would like to test keeping the email copy shorter that way we're more likely to get users to the donation page where they can be presented with more value proposition.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Returning  visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with an 87% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How incorporating claims of value on a matching donation page impacted transactions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that incorporating Bible League Canada’s claims of value on their campaign donation page will increase transactions because the added-value prop language will make the ask more appealing and better aligned with what potential donors care about.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/18/2024 - 04/23/2024
Bible League Canada (BLC) is a ministry organization that provides Bibles and training for local champions and believers around the world. They ran a Value Proposition Workshop to identify their key claims of value that are appealing and unique to BLC, which were then shared with their donors and subscribers in a survey.
The survey revealed that supporters were most motivated by the claims identified in the Value Proposition Workshop.
Bible League Canada follows Jesus’ model for reaching people by equipping local believers to share the Word of God with their neighbors and build healthy, sustainable churches.
Bible League Canada intentionally provides Bibles, one at a time, to people who want to learn more about Jesus through discipleship programs in their local community.
We set out to test whether emphasizing these key claims of value on a high-urgency campaign donation page would increase the number of visitors who donate. Our hypothesis was that if the donation page highlights the claims supporters care about, visitors will be more likely to feel aligned with the campaign and choose to make a gift.
We ran an A/B test in VWO: half of the visitors saw the original landing page, while the other half saw a version with the value claims.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that incorporating Bible League Canada's claims of value on their campaign donation page led to a significant increase in transactions. The treatment group, which saw the page with the value-added claims, experienced a 44.6% increase in transactions compared to the control group. This indicates that aligning the donation ask with what visitors care about can positively influence their decision to donate.
However, the statistical confidence level for these results is 87.9%, which is below the standard threshold of 95%. This means that while the results are promising, further testing is needed to fully confirm that emphasizing the claims of value is indeed the cause of the increase in transactions.
The treatment group also saw a 64.3% increase in their average gift amount ($38.08 vs $33.51 for the control), which supports the hypothesis that the added claims of value make the donation ask more appealing to visitors. This increase had a 86% level of confidence, so further testing is needed to fully validate this lift.
Future experiments should continue testing the impact of highlighting Bible League Canada's value claims on donation pages, such as through new A/B tests or by varying their presentation across different donation pages.

experiments.title: Do Visual Elements on a Donation Page Help or Hurt Donor Conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a simplified donation page design for users visiting a fundraising campaign donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion because removing competing visual elements will reduce mental friction for the donors.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/18/2025 - 07/28/2025
Bible League Canada typically uses a highly visual donation page style as the default format for all of their fundraising campaigns. These pages often include banners, images, and video elements, along with a FundraiseUp donation form embedded at the bottom. While these pages are designed to create an engaging donor experience, we didn’t know whether these visual elements were helping donor conversion, or adding unnecessary distraction and mental friction.
To explore this question, Bible League Canada tested whether removing visual components from the donation page would reduce friction and lead to more completed gifts.
The Control was their standard visual campaign page, and the Treatment was a simplified FundraiseUp campaign page containing only one image and the donation form. The test was structured as a 50/50 split URL experiment during an active fundraising campaign to determine whether visual richness improves performance or adds unnecessary friction.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although we hypothesized that a simplified donation page would reduce friction and increase conversions, the original designed page outperformed the simplified version. The Treatment page saw a 24.4% decrease in donor conversion compared to the Control, with an 89.1% level of confidence—just shy of full statistical validation but directionally meaningful.
In addition to stronger conversion, the Control also generated a higher revenue-per-visitor. However, this may have been influenced by one or more larger gifts, which could skew the average and may not reflect a consistent trend.
This result suggests that in fundraising campaigns, donors may benefit from richer context and emotional reinforcement provided by visual elements like images, banners, and video. Rather than creating distraction, these elements may help reinforce the campaign’s importance and build trust. It’s possible that removing them made the experience feel less compelling or authoritative.
Future testing could explore which specific elements (e.g., video, banners, testimonial quotes) contribute most to conversion. Alternatively, testing a hybrid layout—retaining key persuasive visuals while simplifying navigation and layout—might reveal a better-performing balance.

experiments.title: How a Wiki-Style Dear Reader impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a Wiki-Style Dear Reader on Hour of Power‘s daily devotional pages will achieve an increase in donor conversion because it is more visually arresting and transforms into a sticky bar if the user scrolls past it.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/24/2023 - 04/30/2023
Hour of Power is a Christian broadcast ministry that shares inspiring sermons, ebooks, and daily devotionals through their media presence and website. They have seen success in increasing email acquisition on their website’s daily devotional pages by adding Dear Readers below the devotion, and for their spring high-urgency campaign we decided to see how their traditional Dear Reader asking readers to become sustaining monthly donors would perform against a Wiki-Style Dear Reader.
The traditional Dear Reader appears on devotional pages underneath the devotion. The Wiki-Style Dear Reader appears closer to the top of the page and is above the devotional content. If a user scrolls down the page and goes past it, the Wiki-Style Dear Reader will disappear and a smaller version of it will appear at the bottom of the screen like a sticky bar.
The traditional Dear Reader only has one CTA button which brings the user directly to the campaign’s donation page, but the Wiki-Style version has two CTA buttons. The first button also leads the user directly to the donation page, but the second button that says “Give a Different Amount” opens up a small gift array within the Wiki-Style Dear Reader. The user can then select or enter the amount they would like to give and then they will be redirected to the donation page with their selection automatically chosen.
Our hypothesis was that new placement and style of the Wiki-Style Dear Reader would be more arresting and eye-catching for users on the page and would thus increase donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment did not fully validate, but we saw a decrease in donor conversion on the Wiki-Style Dear Reader with a 90% LOC. What this reveals about Hour of Power's audience is that they do not respond as well to arresting popups/sticky bars that use high-urgency language. Instead, it shows us that their audience is more responsive to CTAs that take a conversational approach.

In future testing we can try using the same kind of conversational language in the Wiki-Style that we use in the Dear Reader to clarify if their audience wasn't responding because of the language used in the Wiki-Style or if it was due the placement/presentation of the Wiki-Style.
