experiments.title: How a simple text email affects click rates on a content offer email
experiments.researchQuestion: Design elements will reduce open rates and click rates.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/10/2021
BRI typically sends emails that have some design elements. So we wanted to test a completely simple text template to make sure that the the design elements weren’t affecting open rates or click through rates. With a recent housefile email offer, we tested the control template against a simple text email.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found was that the design elements were not harming the conversion rates at any level of statistical validity. The click rates were lower for the simple text by 10%, and the level of validity shows a directional lift. However, the open rate was higher by 1.2% for the simple text, but a level of confidence of 59%, which means that life is entirely inconclusive. Lastly, the donation conversion rate on the simple text was 194.5% higher than the designed email, with a 95% level of confidence. And yet, we only had 34% of the donations needed to really find this test truly conclusive. All in all, this test is a mixed bag and shows that we need to continue to test as the file grows, to see if there are any conclusive effects from this style of email.

experiments.title: How value proposition in a handwritten note about the impact of a higher gift affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Showing the direct impact of a higher gift will increase the average gift and revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/11/2021 - 05/09/2021
The Bill of Rights needed to increase its average gift and in turn its revenue from acquisition, that in turn will decrease the net cost per donor. We were looking for ways to increase the average gift without damaging the number of donations. To encourage higher gifts, we devised concise copy that would show the impact and worth of their $50 gift. Then we used a handwritten note to set it apart and catch the reader’s eye. This was run on three donation pages to acquire the needed samples. All of them were acquisition offers for eBooks that were advertised on social. We had to run it for two month, longer than typically advised, to acquire the needed samples.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This tactic worked primarily on desktop and for new visitors. It increased the revenue of new visitors by 55%, with an $11 increase in average gift. However, it decreased revenue for mobile visitors by 35%. Our hypothesis is that the required placement for mobile led to a less desirable user experience. It also slightly decreased revenue for returning visitors, but not at the same rate as it increase gifts for new visitors. The average gift was only $4 lower for returning visitors. It also decreased the number of gifts.

Our hypothesis is that returning visitors who see this more than once are less likely to give, because this message is no longer new, but is instead a turn off. But new visitors, who haven't seen this element before, are intrigued and inspired.

We will roll this treatment out to just new visitors and desktop experience. And we will continue to search for other ways to increase giving for returning and mobile, that also works for new visitors and desktop.

experiments.title: How emotional appeal versus specific outcome affect open rates

rows.name: Don’t lose hope – together we’re making a difference

experiments.researchQuestion: Will emotional language in the subject line prompt more engagement when compared to one that is purely outcome-oriented?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/01/2020 - 08/07/2020
Historically, much of BRI’s subject lines have focused on stating the topic of the email without adding emotional context. From experiments with similar organizations, we have found that using emotional language can help prompt the action that we’re striving for (such as opening or clicking on the email.) We wanted to test this same concept for BRI’s audience.
experiments.keyLearnings: In this situation, we did not see any statistically significant change between the two subject lines. As with most subject line experiments, this same concept will need to be tested over a series of emails to better understand the impact of this hypothesis for the BRI audience.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition and removing an image impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would adding value proposition and removing an image increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/08/2018 - 04/08/2018
Pregnancy Decision Line gets significant traffic to their “Abortion Costs” webpage. Looking at the original page, we hypothesized that adding more value proposition to the page, and removing the header image might increase conversion. We tested the original page against a treatment that had conversational style copy, a larger phone number at the end of the copy, and no image on the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment decreased conversion by 15.6%. Adding conversational value proposition has been known to help reinforce the message and motivate people to take action, but that isn’t what happened on this page. What this tells us is that the people coming to this page are coming to it for answers. Their motivation is high and the additional copy acted more as a point of friction to the decision process than a motivating variable.

experiments.title: How adding credibility and clarity to the landing page affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which landing page produces the the greatest conversion rate to customers for their conferences?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/18/2014 - 03/27/2014
The Acquire The Fire site is a WordPress multi-page installation that gave the user plenty of options: get resources, find out more about the ministry, see the latest list of tour dates, and access to the store (which sold curriculum, merchandise, and other non-ticket items). Sometimes, access to all of multiple pages is a good thing — it encourages users to spend more time on the site and learn more about the ministry. However, we hypothesized that this could lead to what we call “unsupervised thinking” — by allowing users to wander all over the site, we give them more opportunities to get distracted from the ultimate goal. We needed to be able to guide them down the “path of righteousness” that leads to conversion.
We wanted to create an experiment that helps determine what motivates their customers in order to increase their overall conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After just under a month of testing, this landing page delivered a 35% lift in visitor conversion! Additionally, the name acquisition offers grew Teen Mania’s email list size by 13%. This page was a resounding success, and taught us several things about Teen Mania customers:

1. Credibility matters. There are a lot of youth evangelism events out there, but few of them have the endorsements, coverage, and credibility that Acquire The Fire has. This needs to be up front and repeated throughout the value proposition.

2. Buying tickets is a big decision. Our customers are generally buying tickets for a large group and want to make a good decision with their budget dollars. The more that we can show them what they will experience and reinforce that with quotes and testimonials from people who have experienced an Acquire The Fire event, the more we can reduce anxiety about the event itself.

3. Not all visitors are ready to buy. We know anecdotally that it can take time for youth pastors to get budget approval, parental approval, or youth signups. Because of these delays, not everyone who comes to this site is able to buy tickets. We need ways to engage these visitors and give them something of value in exchange for their email address (and the opportunity to market to them later).

experiments.title: How copy on a donation page affects the force of the value proposition
experiments.researchQuestion: By providing a clear and credible reason to give, we can increase both the average gift and conversion rate of the main donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/20/2014 - 04/30/2014
John Cornyn has served as a U.S. Senator from Texas for more than 14 years and is the current Senate Minority Whip for the 113th Congress. As election season begins, a torrent of traffic comes to JohnCornyn.com. This traffic includes likely voters looking to confirm their affiliation, donors looking to support the Senator, and people seeking information about all of the candidates. The Senator Cornyn’s team wanted to make sure that friction and anxiety were minimized on their main donation page and that credibility and clarity were increased so that the most motivated potential donors would make a gift to support the Cornyn campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new treatment delivered a 258.1% increase in donor conversion, with a statistically valid sample size. Team Cornyn learned a few things about their donor base through this test:
1. Experience matters. Even though the donor might be motivated to get to the donation page, they need to be reminded throughout the process that Senator Cornyn aligns with their core beliefs and is worthy of their donation. Donors to Senator Cornyn are thoughtful — they can’t be expected to just react to a donation opportunity, they must be persuaded along the way.
2. Design matters. Simply adding the copy to the page hurt results. Keeping continuity in the user experience throughout the entire donation process is crucial to a positive lift.

experiments.title: How reinforcing the value proposition on a specific gift handle can impact overall revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Will emphasizing the higher gift handle of $50 increase average gift or donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/22/2020 - 08/13/2020
Billy Graham Evangelistic Association wanted to find a way to increase the average gift for the donors that finished one of their pledges. It was hypothesized that one potential way to do that would be to increase the perceived impact of some of the higher dollar value gift amounts. To do that, they highlighted the $50 gift button which would have been a ~20% increase to average gift. This same gift handle was previously mentioned on the page prior to the ask.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for a month and a half, we did not observe a statistically significant change in average gift or conversion rate. One hypothesis behind this is that the value of the $50 was mentioned in the copy right before the form. As a result, this visual emphasis didn’t have the same level of impact it may have otherwise had.

experiments.title: Does expanding the value proposition in the email drive more revenue?
experiments.researchQuestion: Does expanding the value proposition in the email drive more donations for the non-donor audience?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/09/2018
During Hurricane Florence, BGEA experimented with expanding the value proposition within the body of the email it was sending to raise funds for their Rapid Response Team to minister to victims of the disaster. This was the second email in a longer series and was intended to make a direct ask for support. Our hypothesis was that many of the non-donors may not yet understand the reason to make a gift to BGEA and would require more explanation to help them toward a donation. To test this hypothesis, a second email was developed that explained more about the history and purpose of the Rapid Response Team.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a ~60% level of confidence, we learned that an expanded value proposition centered around the history of the Rapid Response Team did not motivate more non-donors to make a gift. However, we also learned that the longer copy did not really negatively impact donor conversion given the low level of significance.
This would indicate the potential for further testing of the expanded value proposition concept but using different reasons to make a gift. There is potential to focus on the increasing need that comes from multiple natural disasters that are coming in the near future or how this ministry fits into the overall mission of BGEA.

experiments.title: How adding clarity to the action affects donors
experiments.researchQuestion: Does making a specific dollar amount ask in an email lead to an increase in the number of donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/12/2015 - 08/14/2015
As part of a follow up to a group of petition signers, the Media Research Center wanted to find what email call to action would lead to the greatest number of gifts.  The standard approach had always been to make a gift request with a link and compliment it with a Donate button.
We had a hypothesis that if we added specificity to the call to action and specifically told them what we would like them to give, we could get more constituents to make a gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment did not reach statistical validity but the results would directionally indicate that adding the “Quick Donate” button motivated a greater proportion of the audience to give a gift. When we add specificity to an action, we are able to remove a portion of the decision making the donor has to do.
We will need to do further testing to validate this approach in the future.
Additionally, when examining the actual gifts made as a result of clicking the button, we found that even if donors clicked a certain amount in the email, approximately 50% ended up giving more on the actual donation form.  This indicates that the value proposition on the page was strong enough to motivate a larger gift.

experiments.title: How the copy on the page affects conversion of a highly motivated audience
experiments.researchQuestion: Will recognizing and thanking the visitors for their previous action increase the donations and revenue to the subsequent page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/20/2015 - 09/14/2015
The Media Research Center (MRC) was running a petition to the major news networks to increase their coverage of the Planned Parenthood scandal. After the initial signature, the visitors were sent to a second landing page and given the opportunity to make a gift. The copy on the page continued the messaging on the initial petition page and targeted the outrage the visitors would be feeling at the lack of media coverage for the Planned Parenthood videos.
After reviewing this copy, we realized that what the follow on page never did is acknowledge the visitor’s prior actions, specifically the signing of the petition. We decided to create a new treatment that would first thank the visitors for signing the petition and then lead in to what else they can do to help solve the problem (i.e. give a gift).
experiments.keyLearnings: After a month and a half of running the experiment, the new treatment produced no statistically significant change in conversion or revenue. It is our hypothesis is that this lack of change goes back to a previous experiment where we learned the motivation of the visitors to this page was extremely high. The copy on the page was not the reason the majority of donors gave so any changes made would not have any significant impact.

experiments.title: How member benefits affected donor conversion during a moneybomb
experiments.researchQuestion: Will member benefits increase the donor conversion or average gift?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/14/2015 - 09/24/2015
As part of their campaign to kick off the election season, the Media Research Center wanted to find a way to incentivize subscribers to become a new member at higher levels. Their standard campaigns have historically not focused on the membership aspect of their gift and instead tried to sell solely the philanthropic aspect of the donation.
We decided to test out language around the benefits associated with membership and include a premium offer of a book that reinforced the messaging of the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was no statistically significant lift for either treatment. Since the campaign was for a limited period of time, we decided to end the test in order to try a different treatment that might achieve a greater lift. It would seem these benefits do not carry the added incentive in the donors’ minds when we have a moneybomb campaign such as this.

experiments.title: How a change in tone of the landing page affects the performance
experiments.researchQuestion: Which landing page treatment will yield the most donors and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/24/2015 - 10/09/2015
As part of their campaign to kick off the election season, the Media Research Center wanted to find the best possible messaging tone to drive an increase in donations and revenue. A previous experiment using member benefits had not produced any significant lift so we decided to take a more radical approach at test the tone used on the page.
Our hypothesis was that landing page that more clearly laid out the problem and emphasized how the potential donor (instead of the organization) could help solve it would convert better. We also removed the 3rd party credibility indicators since, after further consideration, those may have confused the message.
experiments.keyLearnings: Neither landing page was able to produce a significant lift to either conversion rate or revenue. This would suggest that the tone of the message was not influencing the campaign as much as the core message itself. For future campaigns, it would be best to take a more radical approach and test the entire messaging platform before getting to the final days of the campaign.

experiments.title: How the messaging approach affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which messaging approach will get the most donors and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/24/2015 - 10/09/2015
In the final days of the Media Research Center’s high urgency campaign, we wanted to see if a new approach to messaging could increase the conversion rate for the existing donor segment.  The existing copy took a stewardship approach of listing out the recent accomplishments of MRC and crediting the donor for making it happen.
The new treatment removed the accomplishments and instead highlighted recent examples of media bias.  It also added a premium offer for a gift of $100 or more and removed 3rd party credibility indicators that we hypothesized were actually working against us.
experiments.keyLearnings: The radical redesign of the landing page did not produce any statistically significant lift to donor conversion or revenue. Upon further reflection, the donor conversion rate of the page was near 25% for both treatments. By the time the test was launched, the visitor motivation was already significantly high enough that the messaging approaches didn’t have much impact.

experiments.title: How streamlining an admissions inquiry page affects completion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding additional information to a landing page increase the likelihood of completion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/26/2019 - 04/21/2019
Heartlight Ministries has an admissions inquiry page for anyone interested in getting more information about Heartlight Boarding School, that they wanted to increase conversion on. We hypothesized that adding additional information about Heartlight and answering FAQ’s on the landing page would do this. We also streamlined the page and removed extra navigation and sidebars to keep people’s focus on the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment version decreased conversion by 12% (not valid). It’s likely that people are so highly motivated to submit the form once they get there that additional copy actually adds friction to the page, instead of helping.

experiments.title: How a personal donation ask affects conversion on a donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a value proposition in letter form from a known figure of the organization on a donation page increase donor conversion more than a traditional stepped approach with a premium?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/16/2019 - 05/11/2019
Heartlight Ministries was offering a free online course called Bridging the Gap With Your Teen, and wanted to test a previous approach on the donation page that involved a letter from Mark instead of the original stepped donation approach with a premium. This letter from Mark was a much more conversational approach, that told the story of Heartlight and explained that the true goal of the ministry was to equip so many parents, that they didn’t need to run the boarding school anymore.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment option increased donations by 94%. Even though it wasn’t fully valid, this tells us that the message of Heartlight and Mark’s heart for the ministry came through in this copy enough to move people to want to give more than a traditional stepped approach, or the option to get a premium for a donation. This messaging angle, which we refer to as “close the camp”, is a powerful angle that emotionally connects with a potential donor. It is also written in Mark’s voice, which makes a strong impression on donors who heard from Mark in the Facebook ad, in the housefile email, and throughout the course, so they know him and trust him when he says these things.

experiments.title: How a simplified landing page and added imagery affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How does a radically redesigned landing page affect conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2018 - 12/03/2018
Heartlight Ministries is running a free online course called Bridging the Gap With Your Teen, and they wanted to test a new copy approach as well as adding a photo of Mark to reinforce expertise and better connect with the reader. This test was a radical redesign.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment page did not get any valid results based on the low sample size, but the treatment showed a 26% increase in conversion rate and was therefore rolled out 100% to the page.

experiments.title: How using a video in a Facebook ad affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a video of Mark promoting the course convert more people than a graphic that looks similar to the course?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/15/2018
Heartlight was promoting their new free online course, Bridging the Gap With Your Teen, on Facebook. They originally launched ads with a graphic that matched the look and feel of the course, but wanted to test a video of Mark promoting the course to see if it increased conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: Although this test didn’t validate, we saw a 12% directional lift with the video creative. In addition to testing these two creatives, we also tested 3 other stock images and a longer version of the video, which all received a similar or lower conversion rate than these two options. Moving forward, we’ll be testing other elements of the ads to see if we can decrease cost and increase conversion rate.

experiments.title: How the addition of a goal affects donor conversion rate in a high-urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: Does the mention of a campaign goal in a high urgency campaign motivate more people to give?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/03/2018
The Missionary Oblates were running their fiscal year end high urgency campaign and wanted to test whether the inclusion of a campaign goal motivated more people to click through to the donation page and give. They ran this on each email of the campaign , with copy in the emails being largely the same other than the inclusion or exclusion of the goal amount.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment was not statistically valid, but we saw a directional decrease of 11% in donations with the removal of the campaign goal. This shows us that it’s likely more people will give towards a goal rather than an open-ended ask.

experiments.title: How adding a third party testimonial to a landing page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding a testimonial from a well-known figure to a landing page increase conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/08/2017 - 12/18/2017
Heartlight Ministries launched an offer called When Your Teen is Struggling, and wanted to test whether including a testimonial from a well-known Christian author, Joe White, would increase conversion on the landing page. The rest of the copy and elements of the page remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The decrease of 4% on the treatment page was not statistically valid, but it’s possible that the placement of the testimonial distracted people’s eyes from the line of copy, or that the author of the quote was not influential enough to have a strong effect on conversion rate.
This concept would need to be tested again to be a statistically valid learning, and might be more impactful if multiple statements were used.

experiments.title: How a more personal tone in a direct mail letter can impact donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a more personal approach to Direct Mail impact donor conversion and overall revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/03/2020 - 04/16/2020
Back to the Bible has shifted the tone and approach to their digital communication dramatically in the past year. The emails they send to people are very conversational, intentional, and personal. Their email file health has seen a dramatic improvement since this change was put in place. The focus of the emails has been to care for people and their spiritual life. Because of this responses digitally, we were curious how this approach might affect their Direct Mail strategy. To test this, we wrote two different Direct Mail letters. One letter was more traditional in nature – the call-to-action focused on the premium offer and included a P.S. at the end of the letter. The treatment was more conversational, soft, and personal in nature – more similar to the approach we’ve taken in their digital communication. We split their Direct Mail file in half and made sure there was an equal number of donors and nondonors for each variable.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After six weeks of the Direct Mail piece being in homes, we pulled the results of the experiment and found that there was no significant difference in donor conversion. We did see more overall revenue from the treatment, but this is mostly attributed to a handful of major gifts that came in from that variable. We will continue to monitor the results of this campaign over the next few weeks, but we'd also like to test this approach again and take an even more radical approach between the two letters. As you can see in the copy, the main body of the text was the same between the two versions.
Next, we'd like to test taking the digital appeal and just repurpose it for Direct Mail.

experiments.title: How a recurring gift pop-up presented at the time of a donation being processed impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would the recurring gift pop-up increase recurring donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/20/2019 - 06/21/2019
Back to the Bible wanted to increase the number of recurring donors coming from their primary donation page. To do this, we developed a treatment that presented a pop-up at the moment a gift was being processed with the opportunity to convert their one-time gift into a recurring donation. The pop-up had value proposition on it for why they should become a recurring donor and lowered the gift amount to be 60% of what their original one-time gift was. A person would either click, “No, make my original gift” or “Yes, make my gift recurring”.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we weren’t able to validate the experiment, we did see a directional increase in recurring gift conversion of 38% with the recurring gift pop-up. What this tells us is that people are willing to make a recurring gift if we clearly communicate the even greater impact they can have through their gift and give them the opportunity to do it. When analyzing all data associated with this experiment we also found that it did not significantly impact the likelihood to give a gift.

experiments.title: How year-end specific language on a primary donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would year-end specific copy on the primary donation page increase donor conversion during year-end?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/13/2018 - 01/06/2019
During Back to the Bible’s year-end campaign, we hypothesized that we might be able to increase donor conversion on the primary donation page if we used year-end specific language that communicated the overall value proposition of the organization instead of focusing on just one aspect of the organization.
experiments.keyLearnings: We weren’t able to validate the experiment during the campaign. We hypothesize that this is because the motivation of donors during year-end is higher than other times of the year. We see that people are more motivated during the month of December than any other time of year to give and it is more difficult to positively or negatively impact that motivation.

experiments.title: How visually reinforcing a deadline impacts store conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will visually reinforcing the December 25th deadline increase the conversion rate of the site?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/22/2016 - 12/25/2016
One of the most successful fundraising campaigns for World Concern is their Global Gift Guide, especially leading up to Christmas. There is a high volume of traffic going to the site in the week prior to December 25th. With that as a natural deadline, we wanted to see if we could visually increase the urgency of the Christmas holiday.
We created an animated drop down that would appear when visitors first arrived at the site. It contained language that reinforced the overall value proposition of the organization and an animated countdown clock that would remind them of the Christmas deadline. There was no call to action since the Gift Guide has many options for people to choose.
experiments.keyLearnings: With a limited time span available leading up to Christmas, we were not able to validate any significant lift. However, it would appear the drop down may have increased conversion rate if there had been more time to collect traffic. In future iterations of this concept, it is recommended that we find a specific item or gift to highlight to give the dropdown a specific call to action instead of the more general value proposition.

experiments.title: How detailing more value proposition on an instant donation page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more value proposition  for the Scouting survey takers will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/14/2022 - 11/12/2022
The Boy Scouts of America were running a survey to all alumni and people associated with Scouting to gauge their thoughts on Scouting and to try and acquire more names of fellow Scouts.
Following the survey, we had an instant donation page that had minimal value propositions for why they should make a gift to BSA. Because the ICR was low on this page, we wanted to test changing the copy on the donation to copy that detailed their value proposition more and explained better what a gift to BSA would do for Scouting.
The copy we used in the treatment was copy similar to the copy we used on a high-urgency appeal the previous month.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment page saw a 41.9% decrease in donor conversion rate with a 62.2% level of confidence. We had to shut this test off before reaching proper validation because we turned off spend for this acquisition offer.
Because this did not reach validity and only had 11 total gifts, we would like to test this again on another acquisition offer's instant donation page.

experiments.title: Does Identity-Based Messaging Outperform Recipient-Based Messaging on a Campaign Landing Page?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that framing appeal through the lens of the donor’s identity for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion because identity-based messaging increases personal relevance and motivation to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/29/2025 - 10/29/2025
This test ran on Bible League Canada’s Transforming India landing page, which aimed to send Bibles to waiting believers. We wanted to explore whether donor identity-based messaging could outperform the standard recipient-based messaging.
The control version emphasized the hardship and urgency experienced by Christians in India, featuring a clear call to action. The treatment led with a direct appeal to the donor’s faith and identity. The narrative remained the same, but the message positioned the donor as an active participant in Bible League’s mission.
This A/B test ran for the month of October and was split 50/50 amongst website traffic. Visitors came from a mix of meta ads, email appeals, and organic website traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:

This test did not produce a statistically significant difference between the control and treatment. Both versions generated nearly identical numbers of donations, with a 0.8% relative decrease in revenue per visitor. The average gift size was slightly higher in the treatment, but still not enough to come to a strong conclusion. The biggest difference was the decrease in recurring gifts (5 vs 1), but this wasn't at a statistically-significant level of confidence.

Framing the landing page message around donor identity did not outperform a more traditional, recipient-focused messaging. This may suggest that:
There was not a clear enough difference between the control and treatment.
 	The visitors didn't have much of a preference between the two versions.
 	The donors' faith identity was already assumed through earlier steps in the journey
Implications for Future Experimentation:
Testing the identity-based language earlier in the donor journey
 	Combining identity with stronger urgency or social proof to create a more layered motivation
 	Identity framing language in a colder traffic segment where shared beliefs aren't assumed

experiments.title: Static image vs interactive giving element for driving donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that replacing a static image with an interactive gift impact slider for prospective donors  will achieve increased conversions because  showing tangible, real-time impact activates the principle of concreteness and gives donors a sense of agency and efficacy in their giving..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/21/2025 - 09/02/2025
Bible League Canada (BLC) uses story-driven donation pages as part of its digital campaigns, such as DA7 “Transforming Asia,” to engage donors and invite them to place Bibles into the hands of new believers. These campaigns typically pair narrative content with strong visual imagery to create an emotional connection and drive giving.
To test whether making the donor’s impact more tangible would increase conversions, we replaced a static image on the donation page with an interactive slider showing how many Bibles a gift would provide.
Our Control used a static sidebar image of a woman with the handwritten-style caption: “A Bible for every believer.” The Treatment swapped the image for Fundraise Up’s gift array impact slider, which dynamically displayed the number of Bibles placed at each gift level (e.g., $80 = 16 Bibles). The slider was designed to give donors a sense of real-time, visible impact.
The test ran on their Transforming Asia Campaign landing page with a 50/50 traffic split. It reached primarily new donors from facebook advertising, and some warm leads from email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the treatment version using the impact slider intuitively offered donors a clearer picture of what their gift would achieve, the test saw lower conversion performance than the control.
Due to the total sample size falling below the required threshold (3,969) and confidence not reaching 95%, the results are not statistically valid. However, directional insights are still worth reflecting on.
This suggests that, in this context, the emotional storytelling and human imagery may have been more effective at motivating giving than a purely impact-driven interface. One possible interpretation is that the slider introduced a more transactional or analytical tone, which may have weakened the emotional connection that the static image helped to establish.
Additionally, since the campaign itself already emphasized tangible impact in the copy, the slider may have been redundant rather than additive. Future tests could explore hybrid approaches that retain emotional imagery while layering in impact messaging—ensuring the donor’s experience remains both heart-driven and outcome-aware.

experiments.title: How including giving handles on a donation page during a recurring giving campaign impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including giving handles for the Recurring Giving donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because the gift’s impact will be more tangible for the donor. because the gift’s impact will be more tangible for the donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/24/2024 - 11/04/2024
During IMB’s Fall Recurring Giving Campaign, we decided to test including giving handles within the donation page copy.
Both the control and the treatment had the same gift array and the gift array aligned with the amounts within the giving handles. These giving handles ranged from $5 – $75.
This donation page did default to monthly, but one-time giving was still an option.
It is worth noting that this campaign was shut down early, so this test did not get enough traffic to validate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including giving handles in the donation page copy led to a 29.3% decrease in Revenue for All Traffic with a 26.4% statistical level of confidence and a 10.1% decrease in Transactions for All Traffic at a 28.3% level of confidence. While neither of these results are trending in the direction we intended, we did see a 210.8% lift in recurring donations at a 93.4% level of confidence.

The recurring donation metrics was extremely close to validating which does suggest that the giving handles resonated well with monthly givers. It is worth testing further into how giving handles can compel IMB's donor base to give.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that there is enough traffic to validate the results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to further test the impact of including giving handles with different messaging or positioning to see if there is a way to communicate the impact of donations effectively without negatively impacting conversion metrics. It is also crucial to consider the potential effects of messaging on donor behavior and to continuously iterate and test different approaches to optimize donation page performance.

experiments.title: How clarifying the value proposition on an instant donation page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that clarifying the value proposition for perspective donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because they will understand what their donation will do for MAF missionaries and isolated people around the world. because they will understand what their donation will do for MAF missionaries and isolated people around the world.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/10/2023 - 04/12/2023
MAF is running an offer through paid Facebook ads that asks people to leave a note of encouragement for MAF missionaries. On the landing page, they are provided with four pre-written messages that they can select from and then given an optional chance to write in their own note.
MAF wanted to see if we could increase the ICR on the donation page. After reading the value proposition on the control page, we found that the copy was confusing because it went from talking about the work that missionaries are doing to the work that pilots do for MAF. Because of this, there wasn’t clarity on what the user’s donation would do for MAF or isolated people.
To achieve this, we decided to run a radical redesign test and changed the following:
We removed “Thank you” from the headline because we hypothesize that this causes people to feel done with the process and leave the page.
We asked them to stay on the page by saying “Before you go, can we ask you something?”
We clarified the donation ask by stating A) Why missionaries are important B) That these missionaries can only continue this important work because of people who support MAF with a donation and C) What a donation to MAF will do.
We moved the ask for a donation to the end of the copy rather than asking for a donation first then explaining why.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment A page saw a 51.2% increase in conversion rate with a 76.4% level of confidence. It also saw a 108.4% increase in revenue with a 89% level of confidence. The treatment B page (which had the same copy as treatment A) saw a 46.4% increase in conversion rate with a 71% level of confidence, and a 15.6% increase in revenue with a 60.5% level of confidence.

We believe that treatment page A had such an increase in conversion rate and revenue because people would more clearly understand what MAF was asking for on the donation page. Therefore we believe providing clarity increases donations and revenue.

We believe treatment B did not produce as much of a lift because donors didn't have a chance to make a more emotional connection by seeing isolated people in the header image.

experiments.title: How does a cye campaign specific donation page affect revenue?
experiments.researchQuestion: Calendar year end specific language on a donation page will increase revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/23/2020 - 12/31/2020
During their calendar year end campaign, the Bill of Rights Institute wanted to see if calendar year end specific language would increase donations from the direct traffic to their site. We ran a 50/50 split between their usual main donate page and their calendar year end campaign page.
experiments.keyLearnings: We need to continue to test as the sample size was not large enough to reach validation. We will test this again during the next calendar year end campaign. We will also test longer value proposition in the body copy of their main donation page to see how much that increases donations and revenue.

experiments.title: How longer value proposition on an acquisition donation page increases revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Expanding value proposition on an acquisition donation page will increase revenue by increasing the perceived value of their gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/11/2021 - 05/09/2021
The Bill of Rights Institute had been running an offer for an eBook on Equal Rights for months. And the average gift was not as high as we believed it could be. So we decided to test including more copy on the donation page around the need for their gift and the impact it would make. The hypothesis was that this would lead them to give more.
This test ran for two months, longer than typically suggested, because we continued to see fluctuations in the results and wanted to give it a long time horizon.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test did not result in valid findings. When looking at the different demographics. We found that it increased revenue with new visitors by 45% at a 87% LOC, and decreased the revenue from returning visitors by 24% at a LOC of 77%. The findings from these two segments of users negated the other when you view this test through all traffic.

Our hypothesis is that this test showed a directional lift in revenue for new visitors because it told them more about an organization that they are new to, and it taught them more about the value of the eBook they just received. But overall, this test showed that this copy alone was not enough to increase revenue significantly for a sustainable amount of time.

Despite the extra time, these findings were still not strong enough to roll out this test. We will be testing other avenues for increasing revenue.

experiments.title: How does donor centered copy affect revenue?
experiments.researchQuestion: Donation page copy that speaks more directly to the donor rather than about the organization will increase donor
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/07/2020
BRI was offering a new eBook called Equal & Inalienable Rights. The donation page copy was very centered on the mission and impact of the organization. We wanted to test an approach that spoke more directly to the reader and drew them into the mission by focusing more on the benefits to them and to their impact. So we redirected 50% of their traffic to a new donation page with more donor centered copy centered around what they were receiving and why they should give. Donation page copy that speaks more directly to the donor rather than about the organization will increase donor

experiments.title: How a post-donation popup ask affects recurring donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a recurring popup ask after a one-time gift convert more donors to recurring donors?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/07/2019
Jerusalem Prayer Team wanted to get more recurring donations to their Survivor Hope Initiative to give it more regular funding so they could feed more Holocaust survivors. One tactic they wanted to try was asking donors, as soon as they had made a gift, to consider converting their onetime gift to a recurring gift with a single click. They created a popup that fired if someone
experiments.keyLearnings:
Ultimately, the popup did not have any impact on recurring donations. This could be because a higher-than-average percentage of donors selected a recurring gift in the initial step, or because of technical issues with the popup.
Future testing will focus on treatments that cater to the largely mobile audience.

experiments.title: How isolating an appeal from a newsletter affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will isolating the appeal from a newsletter increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/27/2019
Jerusalem Prayer Team had a daily email newsletter that included an appeal, generally combined with a premium offer. However, they wanted to see if isolating the appeal section from the email would increase focus on that section and increase giving by presenting the reader with a “direct ask”. They created two unique emails and split the file, noting that half the file would not receive the news portion for that day.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The appeal-only email delivered 40% fewer donors, though the low overall donor conversion meant that it wasn’t statistically valid. This points to a few possible hypotheses:
First the lack of news content (if relied on by the prospect) could have lowered motivation to “return the favor” with a gift.
The steady drumbeat of news content could have reduced engagement by not offering what the prospect was used to.
This could have just been due to a random sample of the audience having more donor prospects in it than the other, for that particular offer on that particular day.
Ultimately, more testing must be done to increase donation results from the newsletter.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign and donation bracketing affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a radical redesign and donation bracketing affects revenue?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/29/2019
Jerusalem Prayer Team had a successful micro-donation acquisition campaign to raise money to feed Holocaust survivors. However, they wanted to see if they could increase conversion or revenue through additional landing page testing. They created a treatment that centered the value proposition at the top, included a picture of a Holocaust survivor, put the copy on a white background for more legible reading, and then reoriented the gift array. Previously, the gift array offered $3 and $90, with a pre-filled $30 ask in the open field. The treatment page offered the $3 gift, along with a $45 gift and a $90 gift. The hypothesis was that the landing page elements would increase conversion and the bracketing might increase average gift. They launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment produced a 67% increase in revenue that had a valid level of confidence, though with the small overall sample size of donors, this was not fully valid. This showed that the page elements did not, in fact, increase conversion, but that the bracketing did increase the average gift enough to validate the separation between the control and the treatment.

experiments.title: How adding a money back guarantee statement on the donation page affects donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a money-back guarantee message to the donation page will increase donor conversion by reducing perceived risk and building trust.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/11/2025 - 05/07/2025
CitizenGO is a global community that uses online petitions and action alerts to defend life, family, and liberty. Their platform enables individuals to influence decision-makers worldwide by leveraging the collective support of like-minded citizens.
As part of their commitment to transparency and donor trust, CitizenGO has a policy that allows donors to request a refund of their donation at any time, no questions asked. While this policy has long existed, it had never been clearly communicated on the donation page itself.
We hypothesized that explicitly including a money-back guarantee on the donation page would reduce the perceived risk of donating and increase donor conversion rates. By clearly communicating CitizenGO’s refund policy, we aimed to reassure hesitant visitors and increase their likelihood of completing a donation.
This experiment was launched during CitizenGO’s monthly donation campaign titled “FR MD L Expand our MD base in [COUNTRY] 2025.” To ensure broad applicability across CitizenGO’s global audience, we tested this experiment on donation pages in the following languages: English (EN), French (FR), Italian (IT), German (DE), Spanish (ES), and Portuguese (PT).
Control: No changes were made to the donation page.
Treatment: We added the following money-back guarantee message to the donation page:
“Our Promise to You: If you ever feel that your donation isn’t making the impact you expected to defend fundamental human rights, just let us know, and we’ll refund 100% of your gift—no questions asked.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
We ran this test for 27 days and found that overall donor conversion decreased by -9.8%, with a confidence level of 77.9%. While not statistically significant, this directional result suggests that the addition of the money-back guarantee may have introduced doubt or hesitation rather than reassurance for prospective donors. It’s possible that mentioning a refund option on the donation page raised concerns about the organization’s impact or credibility. Further testing could explore alternative placements or softer language to build trust without unintentionally undermining confidence. Here are the results split up by language:
English: Treatment saw a -3.2% relative decrease in conversion compared to control (20.0% confidence).
 	French: Treatment saw a +36.9% relative increase (57.7% confidence).
 	German: Treatment saw a -14.3% relative decrease (59.6% confidence).
 	Italian: Treatment saw a -46.4% relative decrease (90.7% confidence).
 	Spanish: Treatment saw a -15.3% relative decrease (62.5% confidence).
 	Portuguese: Treatment saw a -10.5% relative decrease (21.0% confidence).
Next Steps:
Explore softer, mission-aligned language that emphasizes donor trust without using retail-style guarantees.
 	Test different placements for trust-building language (e.g. near testimonials or impact statements rather than near the gift form).
 	Consider emphasizing positive reinforcement—such as donor impact or satisfaction—rather than offering an opt-out.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacts call volume
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a radical redesign increase calls?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/12/2017 - 01/25/2018
One of the top webpages for the Pregnancy Decision Line is the “Types of Abortion” page. We hypothesized that a radical redesign of this page – removing the header image, adding value proposition to the headline and body copy and connecting personally with the end-reader through the tone of the copy – would motivate more people to call, which is the primary objective of the page. They split the traffic coming to this page and tested the two against each other
experiments.keyLearnings: After six weeks of testing this page, we weren’t able to validate the experiment. There was a slight decrease in calls from the treatment, but because of the test’s low level of confidence we aren’t able to pull any learnings from the experiment or know with certainty that the radical would decrease call volume. We will propose a different angle in testing to better understand the motivation of the end-user.

experiments.title: How a multi-step form impact donation conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a single step form for main donation page visitors will achieve an increase in conversions because previous research suggests that well-optimized single step form out performs a multi-step.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/04/2024
In this experiment, George W Bush Presidential Center had just migrated to the iDonate platform. Their previous donation page used a long-form, single page donation form. When migrating to the iDonate platform, they opted to roll-out a more streamlined multi-step donation form—but they wanted to test the impact of the multi-step widget versus a single step form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that in this specific case, the single step form for main donation page visitors did not achieve an increase in conversions as hypothesized. But with a level of confidence of 52%, the likelihood is that there is no significant difference in performance between the control and the treatment.

Bush Center can move forward knowing the moving to a multi-step for is unlikely to harm results. In fact, there's a chance that it may be the more effective treatment.

With low conversion volume on the donation form, future experimentation should be focused on driving more traffic to the donation page.

experiments.title: LWF – Email Subject Line Test
experiments.researchQuestion: Which subject line will produce the best results for LWF?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/15/2021
Testing subject lines and the preheader. The results on opens and clicks were very similar, however the treatment had a higher conversion rate when it came to donations. While not above the 95% confidence level it does give us something to build upon.
experiments.keyLearnings: The subject line that was tested may have done a better job of attracting people that wanted to get the series as it framed the content as what a person would get out of the book when they read it.

experiments.title: Will more text on a Facebook video ad lead to more clicks to the donation page?
experiments.researchQuestion: Will more copy lead to more clicks to the donation page?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/01/2020
KUOW Public Radio is Seattle’s NPR news station. Their independent, nonprofit newsroom produces award-winning stories, podcasts and events. They were running a campaign and wanted to focus on securing more monthly gifts and hoped a Facebook ad could drive more people to click and go on to make a donation. They’ve seen other Facebook advertising experiments where longer copy has led to increased donations and decided to test it for themselves. In each design, there is a video that was clickable but the main call to action and hope for the ad was to get people to click Donate Now and then visit the donation page where they could continue on to complete a recurring donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Neither the control or the treatment led to any donations and while the treatment had a slight increase in clicks, it was not significant. When looking at total unique clicks, the shorter ad (control) showed a statistically significant increase but the bulk of those were video views so, if the goal was to get more people to watch a video, it appears shorter copy is more effective. It should also be noted that the difference between the control and treatment was perhaps not great enough to prove the hypothesis. Also, taking a donor from Facebook ad to a recurring gift can be challenging.
So moving forward some ideas that require more testing and exploration are:
Shorter ads with videos where the goal is video views or just brand awarenessClearly longer ads where the goal is donations or clicks to a donation pageA short vs. long test with just an image and not a video that can be clickedDonation focused ads asking for a smaller one-time donation as opposed to a recurring gift

experiments.title: How the stories told on a landing page affects name acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Given the altruistic nature of volunteers, focusing on the impact they will have on others will appeal more to visitors than the impact the volunteering will have on them.  As a result, this new treatment will increase the number of names acquired.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/26/2012 - 10/03/2012
The Global Volunteer Network was looking for a way to acquire more applications for their volunteer program.  The existing landing page focused on the experience the volunteers would go through and how it would transform their lives. We wanted to discover what motivated volunteers to sign up so we created a new treatment that talked about the impact they would have on others instead of what volunteering would do for them.
experiments.keyLearnings: We did not reach the required sample size or a statistical level of confidence in the time that this test ran.  While it would appear that talking about the impact they would have on others increased conversions, the lift was not significant enough to be valid.  We would recommend further testing of the idea before rolling it out across the site.

experiments.title: How multivariate copy changes on a primary donation page affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that testing one set of copy variations against the control for primary donation page visitors will achieve a valid learning.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2023 - 01/16/2024
A public policy organization previously launched a multivariate test on their primary donation page in which 36 copy variations were tested in order to find the optimal combination of those changes. These variations included adding a “Your voice in Washington” subheadline, editing copy within a bulleted list describing what the organization does using the donor’s gift, adding “We fight for YOU” copy above the donation form, and more.
After running the test for 11 days, the organization ended the test and implemented changes on the donation page based on the combination of copy variations within the variant that led to the most revenue or donations during that time.
In an effort to solidify the learning on whether or not these copy edits led to a valid increase in revenue or donations, we created a new test with one control, the new donation page based on copy edits as a result of the multivariate test, and one variant, the original donation page copy.
We believe that by testing only one set of copy edits against the original copy, we will be able to reach a higher sample size for the variant and the test will be more likely to lead to a valid learning.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that reverting to the original donation page copy led to a 21.3% increase in revenue for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence is only 79.3%, which is below the accepted threshold of 85% for validity.
This means that the results of this experiment should not be considered valid or used for future decision-making. It is important to conduct further experiments with a larger sample size or longer duration to obtain more reliable and conclusive results.
Future experimentation should focus on testing one variation of copy edits to identify individual changes' effect on donation page revenue or transactions.
